title: Index Laws & Surds
text: Index Laws & Surds Intuition Indices (powers) are shorthand for repeated multiplication, just like multiplication is shorthand for repeated addition.  Surds are irrational roots like  that we leave in exact form rather than rounding to a decimal.  These two ideas underpin everything from algorithm complexity (, ) to floating-point exponents in IEEE 754. Logarithms — the inverse of powers — are covered separately in Foundation 2, Lesson 4. Prerequisites - Tier 0, Lesson 1: Number Systems From First Principles Why index notation exists Multiplication is repeated addition: . Powers are repeated multiplication: . We write  to mean " multiplied by itself  times" (for positive integer ). Deriving the index laws Every law below follows from the meaning "repeated multiplication". Pen & paper:  because . --- Law 8: Combining Laws 3 and 7: Pen & paper: . . --- Summary of index laws Law  Formula  Why ------------------- Product    Count all copies Quotient    Cancel copies Power of power     groups of  copies Power of product    Distribute over product Zero exponent    Quotient with equal exponents Negative exponent    Extend quotient law Fractional exponent    Inverse of -th power General fraction    Combine root and power Surds A surd is an irrational root that we leave in exact form: , , , etc.  We keep surds exact because decimal approximations lose precision. Single surd: Multiply top and bottom by the surd. Surd in a sum/difference: Multiply by the conjugate. Why does the conjugate work? Because , which eliminates the surd: . title: Algebraic Manipulation — Expanding, Factorising, Completing the Square
text: Algebraic Manipulation — Expanding, Factorising, Completing the Square Intuition Algebra is the language of abstraction — using letters for unknowns so you can solve entire families of problems at once.  Expanding turns products into sums.  Factorising reverses that.  Completing the square rewrites a quadratic in a form that reveals its vertex.  These are the pen-and-paper skills that all higher maths assumes you have. title: Multi-Step Equations
text: Multi-Step Equations Intuition An equation is a balance scale — whatever you do to one side, you must do to the other.  In games, think of a character's stats: if a warrior's total attack power equals base damage plus weapon bonus times a multiplier, you need multi-step algebra to figure out the base damage from the final number. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 2: Algebraic Manipulation (expanding, factorising) From First Principles The golden rule An equation says "left side = right side."  To keep it true, every operation you perform on the left must be performed on the right.  Think of a perfectly balanced scale: add a weight to one pan, add the same weight to the other. Step-by-step strategy 1. Expand any brackets. 2. Collect like terms on each side. 3. Move variable terms to one side, constants to the other (add/subtract). 4. Divide (or multiply) to isolate the variable. 5. Check by substituting back into the original equation. --- Worked Example 1 — Brackets and collecting terms Solve . Step 1 — Subtract  from both sides: Step 2 — Subtract 2: Step 3 — Divide by 2: Check: Left = .  Right =  ✓ --- Worked Example 4 — Brackets on both sides Solve . Step 1 — Expand both sides: Step 2 — Subtract : Step 3 — Add 2: Step 4 — Divide by 2: Check: Left = .  Right =  ✓ --- Worked Example 5 — Word problem A rectangle's length is 3 more than twice its width. The perimeter is 42 cm.  Find the width. Set up: Let width = .  Then length = . Perimeter = : Step 1 — Simplify inside the bracket: Step 2 — Expand: Step 3 — Subtract 6: Step 4 — Divide by 6: So the width is 6 cm and the length is  cm. Check: Perimeter =  ✓ --- Visualisation Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Game balance: designers solve equations to set enemy HP, damage, and scaling so that fights feel fair at every level. - Physics engines: rearranging  to find time or acceleration requires the same multi-step techniques. - Machine learning: training a linear model means solving for weights in  — the same algebra, scaled up to millions of data points. title: Inequalities
text: Inequalities Intuition An inequality is like a health bar in a game — you don't need your HP to equal a specific number, you just need it to stay above zero.  Inequalities describe ranges of acceptable values, not single answers.  Every time a program checks if score = 100, it is evaluating an inequality. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 3: Multi-Step Equations From First Principles Inequality symbols Symbol  Meaning                 Example ------------------------------------------------ less than greater than less than or equal to greater than or equal to The key rule: solving is like equations except one thing You can add, subtract, multiply, or divide both sides — just as with equations — but if you multiply or divide by a negative number, you must flip the inequality sign. WHY does the sign flip? Consider a true statement: . Multiply both sides by :   and . On the number line,  is to the right of , so . The order reversed!  Multiplying by a negative reflects every point through zero on the number line, which reverses the ordering. Formal argument: If , then .  Multiplying both sides of by a negative number  gives , which means , i.e.\ .  The inequality flipped. --- Worked Example 1 — Simple linear inequality Solve . Step 1 — Subtract 6: Step 2 — Divide by  (negative → FLIP the sign): Check a value: try :  ✓ Check a bad value: try : , and  is false ✓ Interval notation: --- Worked Example 3 — Unknowns on both sides Solve . Step 1 — Subtract : Step 2 — Add 2: Step 3 — Divide by 2: Interval notation: --- Worked Example 4 — Compound inequality Solve . This means both  and  must be true simultaneously.  Work on all three parts at once. Linear programming is built entirely on systems of linear inequalities. Check Your Understanding 1. Solve  and write the answer in interval notation.  Don't forget: which step requires you to flip the sign? 2. Solve the compound inequality  and sketch the solution on a number line. 3. title: Simultaneous Equations
text: Simultaneous Equations Intuition Imagine two players in a game trading items.  Player A says "2 swords and 3 shields cost 21 gold."  Player B says "4 swords and 1 shield cost 17 gold." You have two unknowns (sword price, shield price) and two equations — solve them simultaneously to find each price.  This is the foundation of every system that needs to satisfy multiple conditions at once. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 4: Inequalities (comfort with manipulating both sides) From First Principles What is a simultaneous system? A system of two linear equations in two unknowns looks like: A solution is a pair  that makes both equations true at the same time. Graphical meaning Each equation describes a straight line.  The solution is the point where the two lines cross (intersect).  If the lines are parallel, there is no solution.  If the lines are the same, there are infinitely many solutions. --- Method 1: Substitution Idea: solve one equation for one variable, then plug it into the other. Step 2 — Substitute  into (1): Solution: . Check in (2):  ✓ --- When coefficients don't match — multiply first Worked example: Goal: make the -coefficients the same. Subtract (2): Back-substitute: , , . Coffee costs £3, tea costs £1. --- Visualisation Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Linear algebra at scale: neural networks solve systems with millions of equations (weights). title: Function Notation
text: Function Notation Intuition A function is a machine — you feed it an input, it applies a rule, and it gives you exactly one output.  In programming, functions work the same way: def double(x): return 2  x.  In maths, we write .  The notation  means "feed 3 into the machine" and get 6 out. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 3: Multi-Step Equations (comfort with substitution) From First Principles What is a function? A function  from set  to set  is a rule that assigns to each element of  exactly one element of . - Domain — the set of all allowed inputs (). - Range (or image) — the set of all actual outputs. - Codomain — the set the outputs live in (), which may be larger than the range. Key rule: one input → one output.  (Two different inputs can give the same output, but one input must never give two outputs.) --- The notation defines a function called  whose rule is "multiply the input by 3 and add 2." -  does not mean " times ."  The parentheses indicate function application, not multiplication. - The letter  is a placeholder — you can replace it with any expression. Evaluating a function Given : Notice the last one: you can feed in an expression, not just a number. --- Multiple function names We can define several functions and tell them apart by name: Then: , , . --- Composition of functions Composition means chaining two functions: the output of one becomes the input of the next. Order matters!   in general. Worked example with a number: --- Inverse functions The inverse of , written , undoes what  does. If , then to find : Step 1: Write . Step 2: Swap  and : . Step 3: Solve for : So . Check:  should give : --- Domain restrictions Not every input is allowed.  For example: -  — domain is all reals except  (division by zero). -  — domain is  (can't take square root of a negative in reals). --- Visualisation Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Functions in code are mathematical functions: def relu(x): return max(0, x) is the ReLU activation function used in every modern neural network. - Composition is pipelines: in data processing, you chain clean → transform → normalise — each step is a function, and the whole pipeline is a composition. - Inverse functions power encryption: RSA encryption applies a function to your message; decryption applies its inverse. Without the inverse, the message stays secret. Check Your Understanding 1. Given , evaluate , , and . 2. If  and , find  and . Are they the same? 3. Find the inverse of .  Verify by checking that .

title: Linear Functions — Slope, Intercept, and Line Equations
text: Linear Functions — Slope, Intercept, and Line Equations Intuition A linear function draws a straight line.  In games, if your character gains a fixed amount of XP per quest, your total XP vs. quests-completed is a straight line.  The slope is how steep the line is (XP per quest), and the y-intercept is where you started (your XP before any quests). Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 6: Function Notation (understanding , domain, range) From First Principles What makes a function "linear"? A function  is linear because its graph is a straight line. -  is the slope (also called gradient) — how much  changes when increases by 1. -  is the y-intercept — where the line crosses the -axis (the value of ). Slope from two points Given two points  and : Worked example: Find the slope through  and . The line rises 2 units for every 1 unit to the right. --- Slope-intercept form: This is the most common form.  You can read off the slope and intercept directly. At :  ✓ (rose by 3). --- Point-slope form: Use this when you know the slope  and one point  but the point is not the -intercept. Worked example: Line with slope 2 through . Expand to get slope-intercept form: Check: at :  ✓ --- Finding the equation from two points Worked example: Find the equation of the line through  and . Step 1 — Slope: Step 2 — Use point-slope with : Check at :  ✓ --- Special slopes Slope     Line looks like           Example ------------------------------------------------------ rises left → right falls left → right horizontal undefined  vertical --- Parallel and perpendicular lines Parallel lines have the same slope: Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals — their product is : If one line has slope , a perpendicular line has slope . Why?  A  rotation swaps rise and run and flips the sign.  If you go "3 up, 4 right" on one line, the perpendicular goes "4 down, 3 right" (or equivalently "4 up, 3 left"). title: Quadratic Functions — Forms, Graphs, and the Quadratic Formula
text: Quadratic Functions — Forms, Graphs, and the Quadratic Formula Intuition A quadratic function describes any quantity that grows (or shrinks) with the square of the input.  The path of a thrown ball, the area of a square as its side length changes, the time complexity of a nested loop — all parabolas. Understanding the three forms of a quadratic lets you read off roots, vertex, and shape at a glance. title: Polynomial Functions — Degree, Roots, and Behaviour
text: Polynomial Functions — Degree, Roots, and Behaviour Intuition A polynomial is a sum of power terms: constants, , , , and so on. Linear functions are degree-1 polynomials.  Quadratics are degree 2.  Polynomials of higher degree model more complex curves — Bezier paths in game graphics, cost functions with multiple local minima, and interpolation of data points.  The degree tells you everything about how many roots and turns the curve can have. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 8: Quadratic Functions From First Principles Terminology A polynomial of degree : - Degree: the highest power of  with a non-zero coefficient - Leading coefficient:  (the coefficient of the highest power) - Constant term:  (the -intercept) Degree  Name  Example ----------------------- 0  Constant 1  Linear 2  Quadratic 3  Cubic 4  Quartic End behaviour For large , only the leading term matters: - Even degree, positive leading coefficient: both ends go up () - Even degree, negative leading coefficient: both ends go down () - Odd degree, positive leading coefficient: left down, right up () - Odd degree, negative leading coefficient: left up, right down () Roots and the Factor Theorem If , then  is a factor of . A degree- polynomial has at most  real roots. Root multiplicity If  is a factor: -  (simple root): graph crosses the axis -  (double root): graph touches and bounces off the axis -  (triple root): graph crosses with a flat inflection Pen & paper: - Root  with multiplicity 2 (touches) - Root  with multiplicity 1 (crosses) - Degree:  (cubic), leading coefficient:  (odd degree, positive → ) Polynomial arithmetic Addition: Combine like terms. Multiplication: Distribute every term. Pen & paper: Verify by evaluating at : LHS: .  RHS:  ✓ Polynomial long division Divide  by : Since remainder is 0,  is a root.  Factor further: . So .  Roots: . title: Rational Expressions — Fractions with Polynomials
text: Rational Expressions — Fractions with Polynomials Intuition A rational expression is simply a fraction where the numerator and denominator are polynomials.  Just as  is a ratio of integers, is a ratio of polynomials.  These appear everywhere: transfer functions in control theory, probability generating functions, partial fractions in calculus, and asymptotic analysis in algorithms.  The key new idea: division by zero is undefined, so certain -values are forbidden. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 9: Polynomial Functions From First Principles Definition and domain A rational expression has the form: where  and  are polynomials and . The domain excludes every  where . Pen & paper: Undefined at  and . Simplifying (cancelling common factors) Valid only when  and .  Even though  cancels, remains excluded from the original domain (it is a hole, not a root). title: Absolute Value — Distance, Piecewise Thinking, and Norms
text: Absolute Value — Distance, Piecewise Thinking, and Norms Intuition The absolute value  answers one question: how far is  from zero? Direction does not matter — only magnitude.  This idea generalises into norms (measuring vector length in ML), loss functions (we care about error size, not sign), and activation functions like ReLU that clip negative values to zero. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 4: Equations and Inequalities From First Principles Definition This is a piecewise function — it uses different rules on different intervals. title: Exponent Rules Mastery — Zero, Negative, and Fractional Exponents
text: Exponent Rules Mastery — Zero, Negative, and Fractional Exponents Intuition Exponents start as "repeated multiplication" — . But what does , , or  mean?  You cannot multiply 2 by itself zero times, negative one times, or half a time.  The trick: we extend the rules by demanding that the index laws stay consistent.  This one idea — extending definitions to preserve patterns — is how mathematicians think, and it underpins everything from floating-point numbers to learning rate schedules. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 1: Surds, Indices, Logarithms From First Principles The pattern that forces the definitions Write out the powers of 2: Each step divides by 2. Note:  is conventionally taken as 1 in combinatorics and CS, but is technically indeterminate in analysis. Negative exponents From . So . Pen & paper: — this is just the reciprocal. Fractional exponents From . So  is the th root: . More generally: . Pen & paper: All index laws — the complete set These hold for all rational exponents, not just positive integers. title: The Cartesian Plane — Points, Distance, and Midpoints
text: The Cartesian Plane — Points, Distance, and Midpoints Intuition Every pixel on your screen has an address: a column number and a row number. The Cartesian plane is the mathematical version of that grid — it lets us turn geometric questions ("how far apart are these two enemies?") into algebra. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 7: Functions and Graphs From First Principles The coordinate system Two number lines cross at right angles.  The horizontal axis is , the vertical axis is .  Their intersection is the origin . Every point in the plane has a unique address : -  — how far right (positive) or left (negative) -  — how far up (positive) or down (negative) The four quadrants Quadrant   sign   sign  Example ------------------------------------------- I II III IV Distance formula — derived from Pythagoras Given two points  and , draw a right triangle: - Horizontal leg: - Vertical leg: - Hypotenuse: the distance  we want By the Pythagorean theorem : Pen & paper: Find the distance from  to . title: Transformations of Functions
text: Transformations of Functions Intuition Game developers resize, flip, and reposition sprites all the time.  Function transformations are the mathematical version: take any curve  and shift it, stretch it, or reflect it using simple arithmetic on  or . Master these and you can manipulate any graph without re-deriving it. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 6: Functions From First Principles The parent function Start with a simple function whose shape you know — for example . title: Exponential Functions
text: Exponential Functions Intuition A population that doubles every hour, a rumour that spreads to twice as many people each day, a savings account earning compound interest — all follow exponential growth.  Exponentials are the fastest-growing functions you will meet, and they appear everywhere in CS: from algorithm complexity () to neural-network activations (softmax uses ). Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 12: Indices and Powers From First Principles What is an exponential function? The base  is a fixed positive number.  The exponent  is the variable.  Compare with a power function  where the base varies and the exponent is fixed — exponentials grow much faster. Key properties (derive by substitution) Property  Why --------------- so   -intercept is always for all   Positive base to any power stays positive As :  (for )  Horizontal asymptote As :  (for )  Unbounded growth Growth vs decay - : exponential growth (curve rises steeply to the right) - : exponential decay (curve falls toward zero) Note: , so decay is just a reflection of growth. Pen & paper: build a table for ----------------------------------------------------- Notice: each step right multiplies by 2.  That multiplicative pattern is the hallmark of exponentials. The natural base Consider investing \n$ times per year: ---------- is special because the function  is its own derivative — the rate of change equals the value.  This makes it the natural choice for calculus, and therefore for all of ML. Pen & paper: compare bases At : , , . Higher base = faster growth. Visualisation Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Algorithm complexity — brute-force search is ; exponential blowup is why we need clever algorithms - Softmax — the function  converts raw scores to probabilities in neural networks - Learning rate decay — training often uses exponentially decaying step sizes: - Radioactive decay / half-life — models with - Binary splitting — repeatedly halving a search space gives , the inverse of exponential growth Check Your Understanding 1. Starting from 100 cells, write a formula for the population  after  hours and compute . 3. Pen & paper: Show algebraically that .  If $f(x) = 3^x(1/3)^x$?

title: Logarithmic Functions
text: Logarithmic Functions Intuition If exponentials answer "what do I get when I raise  to the power ?", logarithms answer the reverse: " to what power gives me this number?" Logarithms turn multiplication into addition, enormous numbers into manageable ones, and appear wherever humans need to compress a wide range of values — decibels for sound, the Richter scale for earthquakes, and bit-counts in information theory. Prerequisites - Foundation 2, Lesson 3: Exponential Functions From First Principles Definition Read: "log base  of  equals " means " raised to  gives ." Pen & paper — translate between forms: Exponential form  Logarithmic form ------------------------------------ The graph — inverse of the exponential Since  is the inverse of , its graph is the reflection of across the line : Exponential   Logarithm ------------------------------------------- Domain: all reals   Domain: Range:       Range: all reals -intercept   -intercept Asymptote:   Asymptote: Passes through   Passes through Pen & paper: table for ----------------------------------------- Notice: the outputs grow slowly — that is the compression power of logs. Logarithm laws (derived from index laws) Since  and exponentiation are inverses: Pen & paper — verify the product rule: ✓ Change of base formula This is how calculators compute any base: convert to natural log () or common log (). Pen & paper: Common logarithm bases Notation  Base  Used in ------------------------- or   10  Richter scale, pH, decibels Calculus, ML, statistics 2  CS: bits, binary search Logarithmic scales On a log scale, equal distances represent equal ratios, not equal differences.  Going from 1 to 10 is the same distance as 10 to 100. This is why we plot earthquake magnitudes and audio levels on log scales — the raw values span too many orders of magnitude. title: Coordinate Geometry — Circles
text: Coordinate Geometry — Circles Intuition A circle is the set of all points at a fixed distance (radius) from a centre. In coordinate geometry we express this with an equation, and then use algebra to answer geometric questions: "Where does this line hit the circle?" becomes "Solve a quadratic."  This is exactly how ray-circle intersection works in game engines and raycasters. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 2: Algebraic Manipulation (completing the square) - Foundation 2, Lesson 1: The Cartesian Plane From First Principles Equation of a circle A point  lies on a circle with centre  and radius  if and only if its distance from the centre equals .  By the distance formula: Square both sides: This is the standard form of a circle equation. Pen & paper: Circle with centre , radius 5: Special case: Circle centred at the origin: . Converting from expanded (general) form The general form is .  To find the centre and radius, complete the square in both  and . Pen & paper: Step 1: Group  and  terms: Step 2: Complete the square for each: Result: Centre , radius . Pen & paper: Centre , radius 5. Radius from origin to  has gradient . Tangent is perpendicular: gradient . → Line meets circle — intersection Substitute the line equation into the circle equation to get a quadratic. - 2 real solutions → line crosses circle (secant — two intersection points) - 1 repeated solution → line touches circle (tangent) - No real solutions → line misses circle Pen & paper: Line , circle . Substitute : → , and  → . Intersection points:  and . Pen & paper: Does  intersect ? →  → Discriminant: .  No real solutions — the line misses. Using the discriminant For line  and circle : Substituting gives . The discriminant  determines: - : two intersections (secant) - : tangent - : no intersection Visualisation Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Collision detection — line-circle intersection = projectile vs circular hitbox - Ray casting — ray-sphere intersection in 3D is the same algebra (with an extra dimension) - Bounding circles — fast overlap test: distance between centres < sum of radii - Regression — fitting a circle to data points (least squares with circle equation) - Signal processing — the unit circle in the complex plane underlies the z-transform Check Your Understanding 1. title: Basic Trigonometry — Right Triangles, the Unit Circle, and Trig Graphs
text: Basic Trigonometry — Right Triangles, the Unit Circle, and Trig Graphs Intuition Every rotation in a game — a turret tracking a target, a character turning, a planet orbiting — is described by sine and cosine.  Trigonometry connects angles to ratios of sides in a triangle, and those ratios turn out to describe circles, waves, and oscillations throughout physics, signal processing, and ML. Prerequisites - Foundation 2, Lesson 1: The Cartesian Plane From First Principles Right-triangle definitions: SOH-CAH-TOA In a right triangle with angle , label the sides relative to : - Opposite (O): the side across from - Adjacent (A): the side next to  (not the hypotenuse) - Hypotenuse (H): the longest side, opposite the right angle Mnemonic: Sine = Opp/Hyp, Cosine = Adj/Hyp, Tangent = Opp/Adj. Pen & paper: A right triangle has opposite = 3, adjacent = 4, hypotenuse = 5. , , Radians vs degrees A full circle has .  But the natural measure is radians: the angle subtended by an arc equal in length to the radius. A point on the circle at angle  (measured counter-clockwise from the positive -axis) has coordinates: This extends sine and cosine to all angles, not just those in a right triangle. title: Function Inverses — Horizontal Line Test, Graphical Reflection, Domain Restriction
text: Function Inverses — Horizontal Line Test, Graphical Reflection, Domain Restriction Intuition In programming, many operations have an "undo": encode/decode, encrypt/decrypt, compress/decompress.  Mathematically, a function's inverse  is its undo button.  But not every function can be undone — if two inputs map to the same output, which one do you go back to?  This lesson answers: when is a function invertible, how to see it graphically, and how to make non-invertible functions invertible by restricting their domain. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 6: Function Notation (covers composition, basic inverses, algebraic method) From First Principles Recap: what makes a function invertible? A function  is invertible if it is one-to-one (injective): different inputs always produce different outputs.  If  implies , then  has an inverse. The algebraic method for finding inverses (swap  and , solve) was covered in F1-06.  Here we develop the visual and structural tools. The horizontal line test Statement: A function  is one-to-one (and hence invertible) if and only if every horizontal line intersects its graph at most once. Why it works: A horizontal line at height  hits the graph at every where .  If it hits twice, two different inputs give the same output — the function cannot be undone at that output. Examples (pen & paper — sketch these): Function  Passes HLT?  Invertible? ------------------------------------ (line, non-horizontal)  Yes  Yes (parabola)  No — e.g.  hits at  and   No (on all ) (cubic)  Yes  Yes (oscillates)  No — repeats values  No (on all ) (exponential)  Yes  Yes Graphical reflection:  is the reflection of  across Why? If  is on the graph of  (meaning ), then , so  is on the graph of . Swapping coordinates  is geometrically a reflection across the line . Pen & paper exercise: Plot  for . Some points on : , , , . Reflect across : , , , . These lie on . Key observations: - Fixed points of  (where ) lie on the line  and are also fixed points of - If  is increasing,  is also increasing - If  is decreasing,  is also decreasing Restricting the domain to create invertibility When a function fails the horizontal line test, we can restrict its domain to a portion where it does pass. Example 1: On all of , this is not invertible (). Restrict to :  Now the parabola is only the right half — every horizontal line hits at most once. Inverse:  (defined for ). Restrict to :  Left half only. Inverse:  (defined for ). Example 2: Oscillates forever — not one-to-one.  Restrict to where it is strictly increasing. Inverse:  (defined for ). Example 3: Vertex at .  Restrict to  (right branch): →  →  → Inverse:  for . Pen & paper: Complete the square on : .  Vertex at . Restrict to : inverse is  for . Domain and range swap When you invert a function: - The domain of  becomes the range of - The range of  becomes the domain of This is why  only accepts  (the range of  for ). Visualisation Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Encryption/decryption — encrypt is , decrypt is ; must be one-to-one - Activation functions — sigmoid's inverse (logit) is used in logistic regression - Encoding/decoding — autoencoders learn approximate  and - Normalisation — z-score transform and its inverse (de-normalisation) - Domain restriction in practice — sqrt() only accepts non-negative input because it is the inverse of  restricted to - Undo systems — every invertible operation in an editor must be one-to-one to be undoable Check Your Understanding 1. title: Basic Statistics — Mean, Median, Mode, Standard Deviation, Box Plots
text: Basic Statistics — Mean, Median, Mode, Standard Deviation, Box Plots Intuition Before probability distributions and hypothesis tests, you need the basics: how to summarise a dataset with a few numbers.  The mean is the average, the median is the middle value, the mode is the most common, and standard deviation measures spread.  A box plot shows all of these visually.  These are the first tools any data scientist reaches for. Prerequisites - Tier 0, Lesson 1: Number Systems From First Principles Mean (average) Pen & paper: Data: Weighted mean: Median (middle value) Sort the data, pick the middle.  For even , average the two middle values. Pen & paper: Sorted: .  Median =  (middle of 5 values). Data: .  Median = . Mode (most frequent) Data: .  Mode =  (appears 3 times). A dataset can be bimodal (two modes) or have no mode (all values unique). Range Variance and standard deviation Variance (population): Variance (sample — use ): Standard deviation: Pen & paper: Data: .  . ------------------------ 3  -2  4 7  2  4 5  0  0 9  4  16 1  -4  16 .  Population .  . Sample .  . Quartiles and IQR Sort the data, then: - Q1 (lower quartile) = median of lower half - Q2 = median - Q3 (upper quartile) = median of upper half - IQR =  (interquartile range) Pen & paper: Data (sorted): Q1 = median of  = Q2 = Q3 = median of  = IQR = Outliers A data point is an outlier if it falls below  or above . Fences:  and .  No outliers in our data. Box plot (box-and-whisker) Shows the median, quartiles, range, and outliers at a glance. Mean vs median - Mean is sensitive to outliers:  → mean = 22 - Median is robust: same data → median = 3 - For symmetric distributions: mean ≈ median - For skewed distributions: use median Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Data preprocessing — normalisation uses mean and std dev: - Batch normalisation — computes running mean and variance of activations - EDA — first step in any ML project: compute summary statistics, draw box plots - Anomaly detection — outliers detected via IQR or z-score - Game analytics — player session length, score distributions, matchmaking - A/B testing — compare means between groups (leads to hypothesis testing) Check Your Understanding 1. title: Piecewise Functions
text: Piecewise Functions Intuition A piecewise function uses different formulas on different intervals — like a taxi meter that charges one rate for the first mile and a different rate after that.  In ML, the most popular activation function (ReLU) is piecewise: zero for negative inputs, linear for positive ones.  Understanding piecewise functions means understanding how neural networks introduce non-linearity. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 6: Functions From First Principles Definition A piecewise function is defined by multiple sub-functions, each applying on a specific interval: Pen & paper: a simple piecewise function Build a table: ------------------------------- Piece At : we use the second piece (), giving . Graphing piecewise functions Step-by-step method: 1. Graph each piece on its own interval only 2. Use a solid dot at endpoints that are included ( or ) 3. Use an open dot at endpoints that are excluded ( or ) 4. Check whether the pieces connect at the boundary Continuity — does the graph jump? A piecewise function is continuous at a boundary  if: In plain English: the left piece and the right piece arrive at the same value, and the function is defined there. Pen & paper — check continuity of our example at : - From the left: - From the right: -  → discontinuous (the graph jumps) The absolute value as a piecewise function This is continuous at : both pieces give 0. Important piecewise functions in ML ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit): Continuous at  (both pieces give 0), but the slope changes abruptly. Step function (Heaviside): Discontinuous at .  Used as the simplest threshold classifier. Leaky ReLU: Like ReLU, but with a small slope for negative inputs to avoid "dead neurons." Visualisation Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - ReLU — the default activation function in deep learning; its piecewise nature introduces non-linearity while being cheap to compute - Conditional logic — every if/else in code defines a piecewise function - Tax brackets — income tax is a piecewise linear function - Game damage calculation — different formulas for melee vs ranged vs critical hits - Gradient flow — ReLU's flat region (zero gradient for ) causes the "dying ReLU" problem; leaky ReLU fixes this Check Your Understanding 1. Pen & paper: Graph the function  and determine whether it is continuous at  and . 2. Pen & paper: Write the absolute value function  as a piecewise function (two pieces). 3. Pen & paper: A neuron uses  (Leaky ReLU with ).  Compute , , . Is this function continuous at ?

title: Graph Reading Skills — Rate of Change, Intervals, and Concavity
text: Graph Reading Skills — Rate of Change, Intervals, and Concavity Intuition Before you learn calculus, you need to read graphs fluently — the way a driver reads a speedometer.  A distance-time graph tells you speed; a speed-time graph tells you acceleration.  The skills in this lesson are exactly what derivatives will formalise later: how fast is something changing, is it speeding up or slowing down, and where does it change direction? Prerequisites - Foundation 2, Lesson 2: Transformations of Functions From First Principles Average rate of change (the secant line) Given a function  on the interval , the average rate of change is: This is the slope of the secant line — the straight line through and . Pen & paper: A car's position is  metres at time  seconds. Average speed from  to : Instantaneous rate of change (intuition) What if we shrink the interval? Interval    Average speed ------------------------------------ m/s m/s m/s m/s m/s As , the average speed approaches  m/s — this is the instantaneous speed at .  The secant line becomes a tangent line. (We will formalise this as the derivative in Tier 3.) Increasing and decreasing intervals Reading from a graph: -  is increasing on an interval if the graph goes uphill (left to right) -  is decreasing on an interval if the graph goes downhill -  is constant if the graph is flat Pen & paper: For : - Vertex at - Decreasing on  (graph falls toward the vertex) - Increasing on  (graph rises after the vertex) Local maxima and minima A local maximum is a point where the function changes from increasing to decreasing (hilltop).  A local minimum is where it changes from decreasing to increasing (valley). For : local minimum at . Concavity — curving up or down - Concave up (cup-shaped, ): the curve lies above its tangent lines; the rate of change is increasing (speeding up) - Concave down (cap-shaped, ): the curve lies below its tangent lines; the rate of change is decreasing (slowing down) Pen & paper intuition: For  (position): - The slope (speed) increases as  increases - This means the object is accelerating — concave up For  (another position function): - The slope (speed) decreases as  increases - This means the object is decelerating — concave down Inflection points An inflection point is where concavity changes — the curve switches from to  or vice versa. Example:  has an inflection point at . - Concave down for - Concave up for Reading a distance-time graph Graph feature  Physical meaning -------------------------------- Straight line, positive slope  Constant speed Curve bending upward (concave up)  Speeding up Curve bending downward (concave down)  Slowing down Horizontal line  Stationary Steeper slope  Faster Visualisation Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Loss curves — during ML training you watch a loss-vs-epoch graph; reading its slope and concavity tells you if learning is progressing - Frame rate graphs — game profilers plot frame time; a concave-up trend means performance is degrading faster and faster - Gradient — the instantaneous rate of change IS the gradient, the core concept behind gradient descent - Animation easing — concave-up curves give "ease in" (slow start, fast finish); concave-down gives "ease out" - Big-O intuition — comparing growth rates of algorithms is exactly comparing how steeply their graphs rise Check Your Understanding 1. Pen & paper: For , build a table at . Identify the intervals where  is increasing and decreasing. 2. Pen & paper: A ball's height is  metres.  Find the average speed from  to .  Is the ball speeding up or slowing down at ?  (Hint: check the concavity.) 3. Pen & paper: Sketch a distance-time graph for this journey: a runner accelerates for 5 seconds, runs at constant speed for 10 seconds, then decelerates to a stop over 5 seconds.  Label the concavity of each section.

title: Polynomial Long Division and Synthetic Division
text: Polynomial Long Division and Synthetic Division Intuition When you divide 157 by 12, you get 13 remainder 1.  The same process works for polynomials: you can divide one polynomial by another to get a quotient and remainder.  This is the backbone of factorisation, partial fractions, and symbolic algebra systems.  In game dev, polynomial division appears when simplifying rational motion curves; in AI, it underpins polynomial kernel manipulations and error-correcting codes. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 9: Polynomials and Factoring From First Principles Why polynomial division exists Given polynomials  and  with , there exist unique polynomials  (quotient) and  (remainder) such that: This is the division algorithm for polynomials — it mirrors integer division. Matches long division.  ✓ Handling missing terms If dividing  by , write coefficients as (insert zeros for missing  terms). Quotient: , remainder: 0. Visualisation Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Computer algebra systems — SymPy and Mathematica implement polynomial long division as a core primitive for simplification - Error-correcting codes — Reed-Solomon codes use polynomial division over finite fields to detect and correct errors in data transmission - Signal processing — transfer functions (ratios of polynomials) require division to decompose into partial fractions for inverse transforms - Cryptography — polynomial division in  underpins CRC checksums - Curve fitting — simplifying rational polynomial models for game physics Check Your Understanding 1. title: Polynomial Roots — Rational Root Theorem and Descartes' Rule
text: Polynomial Roots — Rational Root Theorem and Descartes' Rule Intuition Finding where a polynomial equals zero is one of the oldest problems in mathematics.  Roots tell you where a curve crosses the x-axis, when a projectile hits the ground, or when a cost function reaches a target.  The Rational Root Theorem narrows the search space for possible rational solutions, and Descartes' Rule of Signs tells you how many positive or negative roots to expect — both are search-space pruning strategies, just like in algorithm design. Prerequisites - Foundation 3, Lesson 1: Polynomial Long Division and Synthetic Division From First Principles The Rational Root Theorem For a polynomial with integer coefficients: Any rational root  (in lowest terms) must satisfy: -  divides  (the constant term) -  divides  (the leading coefficient) Why it works: Substitute  and multiply through by .  Every term except  contains a factor of , so .  Similarly, every term except  contains a factor of , so . Worked example: Find all roots of Step 1: List rational root candidates. , .  So , . Candidates: . Step 2: Test each candidate. ✓ →  is a root Step 3: Divide out the factor  using synthetic division. Quotient: . All roots: . Descartes' Rule of Signs Count sign changes in  to bound positive real roots: Signs:  → 2 sign changes → at most 2 positive roots (could be 0). For negative roots, examine : Signs:  → 1 sign change → exactly 1 negative root. This matches our answer: positive roots 2, 3 and negative root . A cubic with a non-integer leading coefficient Find rational roots of . , . , . Candidates: . ✓ Divide by : . Roots: . Fundamental Theorem of Algebra A degree- polynomial has exactly  roots (counted with multiplicity) in the complex numbers.  So a cubic always has 3 roots — they may be real or complex. Visualisation Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Algorithm design — the Rational Root Theorem is a pruning strategy: reduce an infinite search space to a finite candidate list, then test each - Polynomial solvers — computer algebra systems use these theorems as the first step before numerical methods - Control systems — roots of the characteristic polynomial determine system stability (roots in left half-plane = stable) - Game physics — finding when a trajectory hits a surface requires solving polynomial equations - Signal processing — zeros of a transfer function's polynomial determine filter behaviour Check Your Understanding 1. title: Factor Theorem & Remainder Theorem
text: Factor Theorem & Remainder Theorem Intuition If you plug a number into a polynomial and get zero, you have found a root — and that root tells you a factor of the polynomial.  This is the Factor Theorem, and it is the key that unlocks factorisation of cubics and higher-degree polynomials.  Combined with the Remainder Theorem, it lets you factorise by trial and division — entirely by hand. Long division and synthetic division methods are covered in Foundation 3, Lesson 1.  This lesson focuses on the theorems that tell you what to divide by, and the strategy for complete factorisation. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 2: Algebraic Manipulation (factorising quadratics) - Foundation 3, Lesson 1: Polynomial Long Division & Synthetic Division From First Principles The Remainder Theorem Statement: When a polynomial  is divided by , the remainder is . Derivation: Any polynomial division gives: where  is the quotient and  is the remainder (a constant, since we are dividing by a linear factor). Set : Therefore the remainder equals . Pen & paper: Find the remainder when  is divided by . .  The remainder is 20. Pen & paper: Find the remainder when  is divided by . .  Remainder is 5. The Factor Theorem Statement:  is a factor of  if and only if . Proof: This is a direct consequence of the Remainder Theorem. where . If , then , so  — meaning divides  exactly. Conversely, if  is a factor, then , so . Pen & paper: Is  a factor of ? ✓ Yes,  is a factor. Strategy for factorising cubics Given a cubic : Step 1: Find a root by trial.  Try Tip (Rational Root Theorem): Any rational root  (in lowest terms) satisfies:  divides the constant term , and  divides the leading coefficient .  For monic cubics (), try the divisors of . Step 2: Once you find a root , you know  is a factor. Step 3: Divide  by  to get the quadratic quotient . Step 4: Factorise the quadratic  (by inspection, formula, or completing the square). Step 3: Factorise the quadratic. : find two numbers that multiply to 6 and add to . Answer:  and . Step 4: Complete factorisation: Verify: The roots are .  Check  ✓. title: Exponential and Logarithmic Equations — Growth, Decay, and Half-Life
text: Exponential and Logarithmic Equations — Growth, Decay, and Half-Life Intuition Exponential growth and decay model processes where the rate of change is proportional to the current amount: populations, radioactive decay, compound interest, and — critically for ML — learning rate schedules and loss curves. Logarithms are the inverse tool that lets you "undo" an exponential to solve for time, rate, or quantity. title: Sequences and Series — Arithmetic, Geometric, Convergence
text: Sequences and Series — Arithmetic, Geometric, Convergence Intuition A sequence is an ordered list of numbers following a rule.  A series is the sum of a sequence.  Arithmetic sequences grow by adding a constant. Geometric sequences grow by multiplying.  Understanding convergence tells you whether an infinite sum is finite — critical for Taylor series, geometric series in probability, and algorithm analysis. title: Systems of Nonlinear Equations
text: Systems of Nonlinear Equations Intuition A system of linear equations gives you intersecting lines — at most one solution.  But when one equation is a parabola, circle, or other curve, the system can have zero, one, or multiple intersection points.  In game development, this is collision detection: does the bullet's linear path hit the circular shield?  In ML, decision boundaries are often nonlinear curves, and finding their intersections tells you about classifier behaviour. title: Matrices — Introduction, Addition, and Scalar Multiplication
text: Matrices — Introduction, Addition, and Scalar Multiplication Intuition A matrix is a rectangular grid of numbers.  That sounds simple, but matrices are the fundamental data structure of modern computing: a grayscale image is a matrix of pixel intensities, a game character's position and orientation are stored in transformation matrices, and neural networks are chains of matrix multiplications.  This lesson introduces the basics — what matrices are, how to add them, and how to scale them.  Full matrix multiplication and transformations come in Tier 2. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 5: Linear Equations and Graphing From First Principles What is a matrix? A matrix is an  array ( rows,  columns) of numbers: This is a  matrix. title: Summation Notation — Sigma, Properties, and Telescoping
text: Summation Notation — Sigma, Properties, and Telescoping Intuition The sigma symbol  is just compact notation for "add up a bunch of terms."  It is the mathematical equivalent of a for-loop. title: Mathematical Thinking — Problem-Solving Strategies
text: Mathematical Thinking — Problem-Solving Strategies Intuition Mathematics is not about memorising formulas — it is about thinking systematically when faced with a problem you have never seen before.  The strategies in this lesson are the same ones used by algorithm designers, game developers debugging physics engines, and researchers pushing the boundaries of AI.  George Polya called it "How to Solve It": understand the problem, make a plan, execute, and look back. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 3: Fractions and Rational Numbers From First Principles Strategy 1: Draw a diagram Many problems become obvious once visualised. Problem: In a room of 5 people, everyone shakes hands with everyone else exactly once.  How many handshakes? Diagram approach: Draw 5 dots.  Connect every pair.  Count the edges. Person A shakes with 4 others, B with 3 remaining, C with 2, D with 1. Total: . Formula: . Strategy 2: Try small cases first When a problem involves , try  to see the pattern. Problem: How many diagonals does a convex polygon with  sides have? title: Limits — Intuitive Introduction
text: Limits — Intuitive Introduction Intuition Imagine driving a car and glancing at the speedometer.  The speedometer shows your instantaneous speed — not your average speed over the whole trip, but how fast you are going right now.  Calculus was invented to make "right now" precise.  The tool that does it is the limit: we compute average rates of change over smaller and smaller intervals until a single value emerges. Prerequisites - Foundation 2, Lesson 10: Functions and Graphs (function notation, slopes) From First Principles Average rate of change (the secant line) Given a function , the average rate of change between  and is the slope of the line through  and : This line is called a secant line. Pen & paper example: Let .  Average rate between  and : From average to instantaneous — shrinking the interval What if we want the rate of change at exactly ?  Let the second point approach  by choosing  for smaller and smaller : ----------------- 1       3 0.1     2.1 0.01    2.01 0.001   2.001 0.0001  2.0001 As , the expression approaches 2.  We write: This is the instantaneous rate of change of  at . What "limit" means (no epsilon-delta) means: as  gets arbitrarily close to (but never equals ),  gets arbitrarily close to . Key point: we never plug in .  The function need not even be defined at .  The limit describes the trend, not the value. One-sided limits Sometimes a function approaches different values from the left and right. The two-sided limit  exists only if both one-sided limits exist and are equal. Pen & paper example: Both sides agree, so  (even though ). A limit that does not exist , .  Left  right, so  does not exist. title: Conic Sections — Circles, Ellipses, Parabolas, Hyperbolas
text: Conic Sections — Circles, Ellipses, Parabolas, Hyperbolas Intuition Slice a cone with a plane at different angles and you get four distinct curves: circles, ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas.  These shapes appear everywhere — planetary orbits are ellipses, satellite dishes are parabolas, and hyperbolas describe the shape of cooling towers and certain signal-processing curves. Each conic can be defined purely by distances from fixed points and lines. Prerequisites - Foundation 1, Lesson 8: Coordinate Geometry (distance formula, equations of lines) From First Principles Circle — constant distance from one point A circle is the set of all points at distance  from a centre . title: Vectors — Intuitive Introduction
text: Vectors — Intuitive Introduction Intuition A scalar tells you "how much" (temperature, mass, speed).  A vector tells you "how much" and "which direction" (velocity, force, displacement).  In games, every moving object has a position vector and a velocity vector.  In ML, every data point is a vector in feature space.  This lesson builds vector arithmetic from scratch and bridges directly into Tier 2 (Linear Algebra). Prerequisites - Foundation 2, Lesson 1: Coordinate Systems (Cartesian plane, plotting points) From First Principles What is a vector? A vector is an arrow with a magnitude (length) and a direction. Two arrows with the same length and direction are the same vector, no matter where they sit in the plane. We write a 2D vector as an ordered pair of components: = how far right,  = how far up. Example:  means "go 3 right and 4 up". Vector addition — tip-to-tail Place the tail of  at the tip of .  The result goes from the tail of  to the tip of . Scalar multiplication Multiply each component by the scalar : : stretches.  : shrinks.  : reverses direction. Pen & paper: Magnitude (length) The magnitude of  is the distance from the origin to : This is just the Pythagorean theorem. Pen & paper: In 3D: Unit vectors A unit vector has magnitude 1. title: Complex Numbers — Argand Diagram, Modulus-Argument, De Moivre
text: Complex Numbers — Argand Diagram, Modulus-Argument, De Moivre Intuition Tier 0 introduced complex numbers as .  Now we go deeper: represent them as points in a plane (Argand diagram), convert to polar form (modulus and argument), and use De Moivre's theorem to compute powers and roots.  This connects to Fourier analysis, signal processing, and quantum computing. Prerequisites - Tier 0, Lesson 1: Number Systems (complex numbers basics) - Foundation 2, Lesson 6: Basic Trigonometry From First Principles Argand diagram Plot  as the point  in a 2D plane. Real axis = horizontal.  Imaginary axis = vertical. → point . Modulus and argument Modulus:  (distance from origin) Argument:  (angle from positive real axis) Pen & paper: Polar (modulus-argument) form where , .
