title: Dimension, Rank & Nullity — The Rank-Nullity Theorem
text: Dimension, Rank & Nullity — The Rank-Nullity Theorem Intuition When a matrix maps vectors from  to , not all of the output dimensions may actually get used.  Rank tells you how many dimensions the output genuinely occupies — the "effective dimensionality" of the transformation.  Nullity tells you how many input dimensions get crushed to zero.  The beautiful fact: rank + nullity always equals the number of input dimensions. Prerequisites - Tier 2, Lesson 8: Systems of Linear Equations — Gaussian Elimination - Tier 2, Lesson 10: Subspaces: Column Space & Null Space - Tier 2, Lesson 11: Bases & Change of Coordinates From First Principles Dimension of a subspace The dimension of a subspace is the number of vectors in any basis for that subspace. Recall that a basis is a set of vectors that is (1) linearly independent and (2) spans the subspace.  A key theorem says: every basis for a given subspace has the same number of vectors.  That count is the dimension. Subspace  Dimension --------------------- (the zero subspace)  0 A line through the origin in   1 A plane through the origin in   2 All of   3 Rank of a matrix The rank of a matrix  is the dimension of its column space: Practically, rank equals the number of pivot columns (leading 1s) after you row-reduce  to row echelon form. Nullity of a matrix The nullity of  is the dimension of its null space: Practically, nullity equals the number of free variables (non-pivot columns) after row reduction. Pen & paper: full worked example Let  be this  matrix: We have  rows and  columns. Step 1: Row reduce to echelon form. Start with the augmented setup (no right-hand side needed — we are studying the matrix itself): : : : Step 2: Identify pivots and free variables. - Pivot columns: column 1 and column 3 (the leftmost nonzero entry in each nonzero row). - Free-variable columns: column 2 and column 4 (no pivot). Step 3: Read off rank and nullity. Step 4: Verify the Rank-Nullity Theorem. Finding the null space basis (pen & paper) From the echelon form, write the system : Let the free variables be parameters: , . From equation 2: From equation 1: The null space has two basis vectors, confirming nullity = 2. The Rank-Nullity Theorem For any  matrix : where  is the number of columns. Why it is true (intuition): Every column is either a pivot column or a free column.  Pivot columns contribute to rank (they form independent directions in the output).  Free columns contribute to nullity (each free variable generates one basis vector of the null space).  Since every column is one or the other: This is exactly rank + nullity = . The Invertible Matrix Theorem (updated) For a square  matrix , the following are all equivalent — if any one is true, they are all true: Condition -------------- 1   is invertible 2 3 4  The columns of  are linearly independent 5  The rows of  are linearly independent 6 7   has a unique solution for every 8 9  The row echelon form has  pivots Pen & paper check: Our  matrix is not square, so invertibility does not apply.  But consider the  submatrix formed by dropping column 4: Row reduce: same steps give the echelon form with a zero row, so .  Therefore  is not invertible, , and . Visualisation Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Data compression / PCA: The rank of a data matrix tells you how many independent features the data truly has.  If a 100-column dataset has rank 5, you can compress it to 5 dimensions without information loss. - Underdetermined systems in games: A physics engine solving constraints with more unknowns than equations has nullity  0 — the null space represents the degrees of freedom left over, which the engine fills with heuristics. - Neural network weight matrices: If a weight matrix has low rank, it means the layer is not using all its capacity — this insight powers low-rank adaptation (LoRA) for fine-tuning large language models. - Solving linear systems: rank tells you whether a system has zero, one, or infinitely many solutions: - rank  and consistent  unique solution - rank  and consistent  infinitely many (null space gives the family) - rank of augmented matrix  rank of   no solution - Google PageRank: The eigenvector that PageRank computes lives in the column space of the link matrix.  Understanding rank ensures the solution exists and is unique. Check Your Understanding 1. Pen & paper: Row reduce the matrix below and find its rank and nullity. Verify that rank + nullity equals the number of columns. (Hint: look at the relationship between rows 1 and 2.) 2. True or false: A  matrix can have rank 4.  Explain why. 3. Coding exercise: Write a function ranknullity(A) that takes a NumPy array, computes rank via np.linalg.matrixrank, derives nullity from the theorem, and returns both.  Test it on at least three matrices of different shapes. 4. Conceptual: If a  matrix has nullity 2, what does that tell you about its determinant?  Is the matrix invertible?  How many linearly independent columns does it have?

title: Eigenvalues & Eigenvectors — Directions That Survive a Transform
text: Eigenvalues & Eigenvectors — Directions That Survive a Transform Intuition Most vectors change direction when you multiply them by a matrix.  But some special vectors only get stretched or shrunk — their direction stays the same.  These are eigenvectors, and the stretch factor is the eigenvalue. Finding them reveals the "natural axes" of a transformation. Prerequisites - Tier 2, Lesson 7: Determinants - Tier 2, Lesson 6: Identity, Inverse, Transpose From First Principles Definition For a square matrix , a non-zero vector  is an eigenvector with eigenvalue  if: The matrix  acts on  the same as multiplying by a scalar. Finding eigenvalues (characteristic equation) For a non-zero solution  to exist, the matrix  must be singular: This is the characteristic equation. Pen & paper: 2×2 example Step 1: Characteristic equation Eigenvalues: , Step 2: Find eigenvectors For : Row 1:  → Eigenvector:  (or any scalar multiple) For : Row 1:  → Eigenvector: Verify:  ✓ Properties - An  matrix has at most  eigenvalues -  (product of eigenvalues) -  (sum of eigenvalues) Pen & paper check:  ✓,  ✓ Diagonalisation If  has  linearly independent eigenvectors, we can write: where  has eigenvectors as columns and  is diagonal with eigenvalues. Pen & paper: Power of diagonalisation: And  — trivial to compute! Symmetric matrices are special If  (symmetric): - All eigenvalues are real - Eigenvectors for different eigenvalues are orthogonal - Always diagonalisable Covariance matrices are symmetric — this is why PCA works so cleanly. Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - PCA — eigenvectors of the covariance matrix are the principal components - PageRank — Google's algorithm finds the dominant eigenvector of the web graph matrix - Vibration analysis — eigenvalues = natural frequencies of a structure - Markov chains — steady-state distribution is the eigenvector for - Spectral clustering — uses eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian to cluster data - Neural network convergence — eigenvalues of the Hessian affect training speed Check Your Understanding 1. title: Diagonalization — Decomposing a Transform into Stretch Along Axes
text: Diagonalization — Decomposing a Transform into Stretch Along Axes Intuition Every diagonalizable matrix is secretly just stretching along its eigenvector directions.  Diagonalization makes this explicit: rotate into eigenvector coordinates, stretch each axis independently, rotate back.  This turns painful matrix powers like  into trivial scalar powers. Prerequisites - Tier 2, Lesson 12: Dimension, Rank, Rank-Nullity Theorem - Tier 2, Lesson 13: Eigenvalues & Eigenvectors From First Principles The Core Idea Recall from Lesson 13: if  has eigenvectors with eigenvalues , then: Stack all eigenvectors as columns into a matrix : Now compute : The right-hand side is the same as scaling each column by its eigenvalue: If the eigenvectors are linearly independent,  is invertible and we can multiply both sides on the right by : This is the diagonalization of . What Each Piece Does Read  right to left (how it acts on a vector ): 1.  — express  in the eigenvector coordinate system 2.  — stretch each eigenvector axis by its eigenvalue 3.  — convert back to the original coordinate system When Is a Matrix Diagonalizable? Theorem: An  matrix  is diagonalizable if and only if it has  linearly independent eigenvectors. Sufficient conditions (not necessary): -  distinct eigenvalues → always diagonalizable (distinct eigenvalues guarantee independent eigenvectors) -  is symmetric () → always diagonalizable with orthogonal eigenvectors When it fails: a repeated eigenvalue with algebraic multiplicity (times it appears as a root) greater than its geometric multiplicity (number of independent eigenvectors for that eigenvalue). When Diagonalization Fails — Pen & Paper Example Step 1: Characteristic equation Eigenvalue  with algebraic multiplicity 2. Step 2: Find eigenvectors for This gives ,  free. Only one independent eigenvector: . Geometric multiplicity = 1 < algebraic multiplicity = 2. Not diagonalizable. Full 2×2 Worked Example (We found the eigenvalues and eigenvectors in Lesson 13. Now we complete the diagonalization.) Eigenvalues: , Eigenvectors: , Step 1: Build P and D Step 2: Compute For a 2×2 matrix , the inverse is . Step 3: Verify Matrix Powers via Diagonalization Why does this matter? title: Symmetric Matrices & the Spectral Theorem — Nature's Favourite Matrices
text: Symmetric Matrices & the Spectral Theorem — Nature's Favourite Matrices Intuition Many real-world matrices are symmetric: covariance matrices, distance matrices, graph Laplacians, Hessians of loss functions.  Symmetric matrices are special because they always have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors. This means you can decompose them into clean, perpendicular axes of action — no shearing, no complex numbers, just stretching along perpendicular directions. When a symmetric matrix transforms a unit circle, the result is always an axis-aligned ellipse whose axes point along the eigenvectors. Prerequisites - Tier 2, Lesson 13: Eigenvalues & Eigenvectors - Tier 2, Lesson 6: Identity, Inverse, Transpose From First Principles What is a symmetric matrix? A matrix  is symmetric if , meaning for all .  It is mirrored across the main diagonal: Only square matrices can be symmetric.  The diagonal entries are unrestricted; the off-diagonal entries must satisfy . Where symmetric matrices arise - Covariance matrix:  — always symmetric - Hessian: second partial derivatives commute, so - Graph Laplacian:  for undirected graphs - Quadratic forms: any expression  can use a symmetric The Spectral Theorem (real case) Theorem: Every real symmetric matrix  satisfies: 1. All eigenvalues are real numbers 2. Eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal 3.  can be orthogonally diagonalised: where  is an orthogonal matrix () whose columns are unit eigenvectors, and  is diagonal with eigenvalues. Why eigenvalues are real (sketch): Suppose . Take the conjugate transpose of both sides: . Since , multiply the original equation on the left by : Since , we get , so  is real. Why eigenvectors are orthogonal: Let and  with . So .  Since , we must have  — orthogonal. Verify by hand: ✓ Quadratic forms and ellipses A quadratic form is . For our example: The level set  is an ellipse.  The Spectral Theorem tells us the eigenvectors are the axes of the ellipse, and the eigenvalues determine the radii: . In the eigenvector coordinate system (rotated by ), the cross terms vanish and we get  — a clean axis-aligned ellipse. Visualisation Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - PCA — the covariance matrix is symmetric; its eigenvectors (principal components) are guaranteed orthogonal by the Spectral Theorem - Hessian analysis — the Hessian of a loss function is symmetric; its eigenvalues tell you if a critical point is a minimum (all positive), maximum (all negative), or saddle point (mixed) - Spectral clustering — uses eigenvectors of the symmetric graph Laplacian to embed nodes before clustering - Physics engines — inertia tensors are symmetric; eigenvectors give the principal axes of rotation - Quadratic forms in optimisation —  with positive eigenvalues means a convex bowl — gradient descent will converge Check Your Understanding 1. title: Norms — L1, L2, Lp, Frobenius
text: Norms — L1, L2, Lp, Frobenius Intuition A norm is a way to measure the "size" of a vector or matrix.  Different norms measure size differently — like how "distance" in Manhattan (walking on a grid) differs from "distance" as the crow flies.  In ML, the choice of norm directly affects regularisation, loss functions, and robustness. Prerequisites - Tier 2, Lesson 2: Vector Operations (magnitude) From First Principles What is a norm? A norm  is any function that satisfies: 1. , and  iff  (non-negative) 2.  (scales with scalar) 3.  (triangle inequality) The  norm L1 norm (Manhattan distance) Pen & paper: Think of it as walking on a grid: you can only move along axes. L2 norm (Euclidean distance) Pen & paper: This is the "straight line" distance — the one from Pythagoras. L∞ norm (max norm) Pen & paper: Just take the largest absolute value. Unit circles under different norms The "unit circle"  in 2D: Norm  Shape ------------- Diamond (rotated square) Circle Square As  increases, the unit circle goes from diamond → circle → square. Frobenius norm (for matrices) It's just the L2 norm if you flatten the matrix into a vector. Pen & paper: Norms and regularisation in ML Regularisation  Norm  Effect on weights ---------------------------------------- L1 (Lasso)    Drives weights to exactly 0 → feature selection L2 (Ridge)    Drives weights toward 0 but not exactly → smooth, small weights Elastic Net    Combines both Why L1 gives sparsity (pen & paper intuition): The L1 unit "circle" is a diamond.  When you find where the loss contours first touch the diamond constraint, they tend to hit a corner — and corners are on the axes, where at least one coordinate is 0. Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Loss functions — MSE uses L2, MAE uses L1; L2 penalises outliers more - Regularisation — L1 (Lasso) for sparse models, L2 (Ridge) for smooth models - Batch normalisation — normalises activations using L2 norm - Word embeddings — cosine similarity uses L2-normalised vectors - Adversarial attacks — perturbations measured in L∞ ("no pixel changes by more than ε") - Game dev — L2 distance for physics, L∞ for checking if something is "within a box" Check Your Understanding 1. Think about it: In adversarial ML, attacks are bounded by .  Why L∞ and not L2?  What real-world constraint does this model?

title: Orthogonality and the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality
text: Orthogonality and the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality Intuition Two vectors are orthogonal when they point in completely independent directions — changing one has zero effect on the other.  The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality tells you the maximum the dot product can ever be, and orthogonal matrices are transforms that preserve all distances and angles — the rigid motions used in 3D game engines. Prerequisites - Tier 2, Lesson 3: Dot Product Geometry () - Tier 2, Lesson 16: Norms (L2 norm, ) From First Principles 1. If additionally every vector has unit length (), the set is orthonormal. 3. Cauchy-Schwarz inequality — derivation Statement: For any vectors  in : Proof. Since  for all , the quadratic has at most one real root, so its discriminant must be : Take square roots: Equality holds when  for some scalar  (i.e. the vectors are parallel). title: Orthogonal Projections
text: Orthogonal Projections Intuition Imagine the sun is directly overhead and you cast a shadow onto the floor. That shadow is the projection of your 3-D body onto a 2-D plane.  In linear algebra, projecting a vector onto a line or subspace finds the closest point in that subspace — the "shadow."  This idea is the engine behind least-squares fitting, PCA, and every regression model you will ever train. Prerequisites - Tier 2, Lesson 3: Dot Product — Geometry (angle, inner product) - Tier 2, Lesson 6: Identity, Inverse, Transpose - Tier 2, Lesson 16: Norms - Tier 2, Lesson 17: Orthogonality & Cauchy-Schwarz (orthogonal complement) From First Principles 1. Projection of a vector onto a line We have vectors  and  (). We want the point on the line through  that is closest to .  That point must be some scalar multiple . The residual  must be orthogonal to (shortest distance is always perpendicular): Expand: Solve for : Therefore the projection vector is: = \hat{c}\,\mathbf{u} = \frac{\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{u}}{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u}}\,\mathbf{u}$$ Pen-and-paper example. Let , . Step 1 — numerator: Step 2 — denominator: Step 3 — scalar: Step 4 — projection: Step 5 — residual: Step 6 — verify orthogonality: 2. Projection onto a subspace Let  be a subspace with basis vectors as columns of matrix (shape ,  columns =  basis vectors). We seek  such that  is the closest point in  to .  The residual must be orthogonal to every column of , i.e.: Expand: Solve (these are the normal equations): \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \hat{\mathbf{x}} = (\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{v}$$ The projected vector is: = \mathbf{A}\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}(\mathbf{A}^T\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{A}^T\,\mathbf{v}$$ 3. The projection matrix Define: Property 1 — Idempotent: . Proof: . The middle  cancels its inverse: . Projecting twice does nothing extra — you are already on the subspace. Property 2 — Symmetric: . Proof: . Since  is symmetric, so is its inverse, giving . 4. Pen-and-paper example — projection onto a plane in R^3 Let  (the xy-plane) and . Fitting a line through data is a projection problem. - PCA projects high-dimensional data onto the subspace of the top eigenvectors of the covariance matrix. - Shadow rendering in games is literally projecting 3-D geometry onto a 2-D surface. - Gram-Schmidt (next lesson) repeatedly subtracts projections to build orthogonal bases, which power QR decomposition and stable numerical solvers. title: The Gram-Schmidt Process & QR Decomposition
text: The Gram-Schmidt Process & QR Decomposition Intuition You have a set of basis vectors that are "skewed" — not perpendicular to each other.  Gram-Schmidt straightens them out one at a time, like building a set of perfectly perpendicular rulers.  Each new ruler is made by taking the next vector and subtracting off its projections onto all the rulers you have already built.  The result is an orthogonal (or orthonormal) basis — the foundation of QR decomposition, which is how modern software solves least-squares problems and computes eigenvalues stably. Prerequisites - Tier 2, Lesson 18: Orthogonal Projections (projection formula) - Tier 2, Lesson 16: Norms (vector length / normalisation) - Tier 2, Lesson 9: Linear Combinations, Span, Independence From First Principles The problem Given linearly independent vectors , produce orthogonal vectors that span the same subspace. The algorithm Step 1. Keep the first vector as-is: Step 2. Subtract from  its projection onto : Now . Step 3. Subtract from  its projections onto both  and : - \frac{\mathbf{a}3 \cdot \mathbf{u}1}{\mathbf{u}1 \cdot \mathbf{u}1}\,\mathbf{u}1 - \frac{\mathbf{a}3 \cdot \mathbf{u}2}{\mathbf{u}2 \cdot \mathbf{u}2}\,\mathbf{u}2$$ General step : To get an orthonormal basis, normalise each vector: Pen-and-paper example (3 vectors in R^3) Let , , . u1: u2: Check: u3: Projection onto : Projection onto : Check: Check: Connection to QR Decomposition Arrange the orthonormal vectors  as columns of (an  matrix with orthonormal columns, ). Since we built each  from the , we can write: The coefficients  form an upper triangular matrix . In matrix form: This is the QR decomposition.  Gram-Schmidt is one way to compute it. Visualisation Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - QR decomposition is the standard numerical method for solving least-squares problems — more stable than forming  directly (which squares the condition number). - QR iteration (repeatedly doing QR decomposition) is how eigenvalue algorithms work inside NumPy and LAPACK. - Orthonormal bases simplify everything: projection becomes a single dot product, coordinates decouple, and numerical errors do not cascade. - In 3-D game engines, orthonormalising a camera's local axes (forward, right, up) prevents the axes from drifting out of alignment due to accumulated floating-point errors. - Signal processing: orthogonal basis functions (sines, cosines, wavelets) let you decompose a signal into independent components — Gram-Schmidt generalises this idea. Check Your Understanding 1. Apply Gram-Schmidt by hand to , . Verify the result is orthogonal. 2. Why is the matrix  upper triangular? (Hint: think about which  exist at the time you process .) 3. What happens if you feed Gram-Schmidt a set of linearly dependent vectors? At which step does it break, and how could you detect this in code?

title: Quadratic Forms, Definiteness & the Pseudoinverse
text: Quadratic Forms, Definiteness & the Pseudoinverse Intuition When a neural network's loss surface curves like a bowl, gradient descent rolls smoothly to the bottom — that is a positive definite Hessian.  When the surface is saddle-shaped, some directions go up and others go down — that is indefinite.  Quadratic forms give us a single number that captures how a matrix "curves" space, and definiteness tells us the shape of that curvature. Understanding this is the key to knowing whether an optimiser is converging, whether a covariance matrix is valid, or whether a quadratic constraint in a game engine describes an ellipse or a hyperbola. Prerequisites - Tier 2, Lesson 15: Symmetric Matrices & Spectral Theorem (eigendecomposition of symmetric matrices) - Tier 2, Lesson 19: Gram-Schmidt Process (orthonormal bases, QR) From First Principles Bilinear forms A bilinear form takes two vectors and produces a scalar, linear in each argument separately: where  is an  matrix.  For : The dot product is a special case where . Quadratic forms Set  in the bilinear form: For a  symmetric matrix , expand by hand: Step 1. Multiply : Step 2. Dot with : Note the  cross-term — both off-diagonal entries contribute once each. We can always assume  is symmetric because for any matrix , . Definiteness — three types Let  be a real symmetric  matrix with eigenvalues . Type  Eigenvalue condition  Shape of  level set (2D) ------------------------------------------------------------ Positive definite (PD)  All   Ellipse (bowl up) Negative definite (ND)  All   Ellipse (bowl down) Indefinite  Mixed signs  Hyperbola (saddle) There are also positive semi-definite () and negative semi-definite () — the "semi" means some eigenvalues can be zero. Pen-and-paper examples Example 1 — Positive definite: Eigenvalues: . Both positive, so  is PD.  The quadratic form  for all . Example 2 — Indefinite: Eigenvalues: . Mixed signs, so  is indefinite.  The form  takes both positive and negative values. The leading minors test (Sylvester's criterion) For a symmetric , check the leading principal minors — the determinants of the top-left  submatrices: - PD if and only if all leading minors are positive: , , etc. - ND if and only if the minors alternate in sign: , second minor , third , ... Verify Example 1: .  .  Both positive — PD confirmed. Verify Example 2: .  .  Fails both PD and ND tests — indefinite confirmed. Change of variables: diagonalising the quadratic form By the Spectral Theorem (Lesson 15), any real symmetric matrix decomposes as . Substitute  (rotate coordinates to eigenvector axes): The cross-terms vanish.  In the new coordinates, the level sets are axis-aligned ellipses (if PD) or hyperbolas (if indefinite). Connection to conics The equation  defines: - An ellipse when  is PD (semi-axes ) - A hyperbola when  is indefinite The Moore-Penrose Pseudoinverse When  is not invertible (e.g. it is rectangular or singular), the pseudoinverse  generalises the inverse.  Given the SVD : where  is formed by taking the reciprocal of each non-zero singular value and transposing the matrix.  For a  matrix with , we get (now ). The pseudoinverse gives the minimum-norm least-squares solution to : . Visualisation The contour plot below shows the level sets of  for a positive definite matrix (ellipses) versus an indefinite matrix (hyperbolas). Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Hessian definiteness in optimisation: a PD Hessian at a critical point guarantees a local minimum — gradient descent converges.  An indefinite Hessian means a saddle point: the optimiser must escape it. - Covariance matrices are always positive semi-definite.  If your computed covariance matrix has a negative eigenvalue, you have a numerical bug. - Collision detection: testing whether a point lies inside an ellipsoid is exactly checking  for a PD matrix. - Pseudoinverse powers least-squares fitting, underdetermined systems in robotics/IK, and the normal equation  when is singular.  np.linalg.lstsq uses it internally. - Kernel methods in ML: the kernel matrix (Gram matrix) must be PSD for the kernel to correspond to a valid inner product in feature space. Check Your Understanding 1. By hand: Given , compute the eigenvalues and classify the definiteness.  What does the level set  look like? 2. Sylvester's criterion: For , check the leading principal minors and determine the definiteness without computing eigenvalues. 3. Coding exercise: Compute the pseudoinverse of by hand using the SVD, then verify with np.linalg.pinv.

title: SVD — Singular Value Decomposition
text: SVD — Singular Value Decomposition Intuition Any matrix — even non-square ones — can be broken into three simple steps: rotate → stretch → rotate.  That's the SVD.  It's the Swiss Army knife of linear algebra: image compression, noise removal, recommendation systems, and the mathematical foundation of PCA all use SVD. Prerequisites - Tier 2, Lesson 13: Eigenvalues & Eigenvectors From First Principles The decomposition Every  matrix  can be written as: Matrix  Shape  What it is -------------------------- Left singular vectors (orthogonal) — rotation in output space Diagonal with singular values  — stretching Right singular vectors (orthogonal) — rotation in input space How to compute SVD by hand (2×2) This is already diagonal, so SVD is trivial: , , Singular values: . Step 1: Compute  (2×2): Step 2: Eigenvalues of : → , Singular values: , Step 3: Eigenvectors of  give  (2×2): For :  →  → For :  →  → Step 4:  is 3×2 (same shape as ): Step 5: Compute  columns from : For the full  (3×3), we need a third column  orthogonal to both. Using Gram–Schmidt or inspection: Verify:  should recover . The third column of  is multiplied by the zero row of , so it doesn't contribute — which brings us to economy SVD. Economy SVD vs full SVD Full SVD  Economy (thin) SVD ------------------------------ (padded with zeros) Here  (or the rank, if truncated). For our 3×2 example, the economy SVD is: , ,  stays 2×2. This is what NumPy's np.linalg.svd(A, fullmatrices=False) returns. The economy form saves storage: for a 1000×50 matrix,  is 1000×50 instead of 1000×1000. Low-rank approximation Keep only the top  singular values: This is the best rank- approximation of  (in Frobenius norm). This is how image compression works: a 1000×1000 image matrix might be well-approximated using only 50 singular values, compressing it 20×. Relationship to eigendecomposition - Singular values of  = square roots of eigenvalues of - For symmetric positive definite : SVD = eigendecomposition Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Image compression — keep top- singular values, discard the rest - Recommendation systems — Netflix prize: SVD on the user-movie rating matrix - PCA — derived from SVD of the data matrix (next lesson) - Latent Semantic Analysis — NLP technique using SVD on term-document matrices - Pseudoinverse —  (used in least squares) Check Your Understanding 1. title: PCA — Principal Component Analysis from SVD
text: PCA — Principal Component Analysis from SVD Intuition You have data with 100 features, but most of the "interesting variation" lives in just 3–5 directions.  PCA finds those directions — the axes along which the data varies the most.  It's dimensionality reduction: throw away the noise, keep the signal. Prerequisites - Tier 2, Lesson 21: SVD From First Principles The goal Given data matrix  ( samples ×  features), find directions that capture the most variance. Step-by-step derivation (pen & paper walkthrough) Sample data: 4 points in 2D Point ----------------------- A  2  1 B  4  3 C  6  5 D  8  7 Step 1: Centre the data (subtract mean) Mean: Point ----------------- A  -3  -3 B  -1  -1 C  1  1 D  3  3 Step 2: Compute covariance matrix Step 3: Find eigenvalues/eigenvectors of , For : Interpretation: All the variance lies along the direction  — the data is perfectly on a line!   means zero variance in the perpendicular direction. Step 4: Project onto principal components Variance explained In our example: PC1 captures  of the variance. Worked example 2: Non-trivial data (both eigenvalues nonzero) Sample data: 4 points in 2D with variance in two directions Point ----------------------- A  2  3 B  4  5 C  6  4 D  8  8 Step 1: Centre the data Mean: , Point ----------------- A  -3  -2 B  -1  0 C  1  -1 D  3  3 Step 2: Covariance matrix Step 3: Eigenvalues , Both eigenvalues are nonzero — the data has variance in two directions (it does not lie on a perfect line). Step 4: Variance explained PC1: PC2: Interpretation: PC1 captures 92% of the variance. If we reduce to 1D, we lose only 8% of the information. Unlike Example 1 (perfectly collinear data), here we genuinely have some spread in the second direction — so the "explained variance" metric becomes meaningful for deciding how many components to keep. PCA via SVD (the practical method) Instead of computing the covariance matrix, use SVD directly on : - Columns of  = principal components (eigenvectors of ) -  = eigenvalues of  (variance along each PC) This is numerically more stable and how sklearn.PCA works internally. Choosing Common rules: - Keep enough PCs to explain 95% of variance - Look for an "elbow" in the scree plot (singular values vs index) Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Dimensionality reduction — reduce 1000 features to 50 while keeping 95% of information - Face recognition — "eigenfaces" are the principal components of face images - Noise removal — low-rank reconstruction via PCA discards noisy dimensions - Visualisation — project high-dimensional data to 2D/3D for plotting - Feature engineering — PCA components as new features for ML models Check Your Understanding 1. Pen & paper: Given centred data , compute the covariance matrix. 2. Pen & paper: If the eigenvalues of a covariance matrix are , how much variance does the first PC capture? 3. Think about it: Why must data be centred before PCA?  What goes wrong if you don't?

title: Limits — The Foundation of Calculus
text: Limits — The Foundation of Calculus Intuition A limit asks: "What value does  approach as  gets closer and closer to some point?"  You might never actually reach that point, but you can get arbitrarily close.  Limits are the rigorous way to handle "approaching" — and they underpin derivatives, integrals, and everything in calculus. Prerequisites - Tier 0: Number Systems (real numbers) - Tier 1, Lesson 3: Relations and Functions From First Principles Informal definition means: as  gets closer to  (but ),  gets closer to . Pen & paper: Just plug in — for polynomials, this always works. When direct substitution fails Plugging in gives  — indeterminate.  But try values approaching 0: -------------------- 1.0  0.8415 0.1  0.9983 0.01  0.99998 0.001  0.9999998 The limit is 1. Algebraic tricks for evaluating limits (pen & paper) Trick 1: Factor and cancel Trick 2: Multiply by conjugate Multiply top and bottom by the conjugate : The numerator uses the difference of squares: : Trick 3: Divide by highest power Divide top and bottom by : The epsilon-delta definition means: for every , there exists  such that In plain English: "however close you want  to be to  (), I can find a neighbourhood around  () that guarantees it." Pen & paper: Prove Need: , i.e., So choose . If , then  ✓ One-sided limits The two-sided limit exists only if both one-sided limits exist and are equal. Important limits to memorise Continuity A function  is continuous at  if: 1.  is defined 2.  exists 3. ReLU is continuous everywhere. Sigmoid is continuous. Step function is not continuous at 0. Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Numerical analysis — floating-point operations are limits in disguise (finite approximations to real arithmetic) - Learning rate schedules — many approach 0 as training progresses (a limit) - Convergence — gradient descent converges if the loss "limits" to a minimum - Series approximation — Taylor series (Tier 3-09) requires limits to define convergence - Probability — the law of large numbers is a limit theorem Check Your Understanding 1. title: Derivatives — Slope of the Tangent
text: Derivatives — Slope of the Tangent Intuition The derivative tells you how fast something is changing at a specific instant.  Speed is the derivative of position.  The slope of a curve at a point is the derivative at that point.  In ML, the derivative tells the optimiser which way to adjust weights to reduce the loss. Prerequisites - Tier 3, Lesson 1: Limits From First Principles The limit definition This is the slope of the secant line as it becomes the tangent. Pen & paper: Derive Pen & paper: Derive First expand  using the binomial formula: Now substitute: The power rule (general) This works for any real , not just integers. title: Partial Derivatives and the Gradient ∇
text: Partial Derivatives and the Gradient ∇ Intuition When a function has multiple inputs (like a loss function depending on many weights), the partial derivative tells you how the output changes when you tweak one input while holding the rest fixed.  The gradient bundles all partial derivatives into a vector pointing in the direction of steepest ascent.  Flip the sign and you get gradient descent. That's the chain rule.  It's the single most important derivative rule for deep learning because backpropagation is just the chain rule applied repeatedly. title: Jacobian and Hessian Matrices
sections: Matrix Calculus: Vector and Matrix Gradients
text: Jacobian and Hessian Matrices Intuition The Jacobian generalises the derivative to functions with multiple inputs and outputs — it's the matrix of all first-order partial derivatives.  The Hessian is the matrix of all second-order partial derivatives, and it tells you about the curvature of a function: is this critical point a bowl (minimum), a hill (maximum), or a saddle? Prerequisites - Tier 3, Lesson 3: Partial Derivatives and Gradient - Tier 2, Lesson 4: Matrix Multiplication From First Principles The Jacobian matrix For a function  with  outputs and  inputs: Row  = gradient of the -th output. Pen & paper: At : Jacobian determinant For square Jacobians (), the determinant tells you how much the transformation locally scales area/volume. This appears in change of variables for integration (next few lessons). The Hessian matrix For a scalar function , the Hessian is the  matrix of second partial derivatives: The Hessian is always symmetric:  (this is Clairaut's theorem — the order of differentiation doesn't matter, as long as the second derivatives are continuous). Pen & paper: First derivatives: , Second derivatives: At : Classifying critical points with the Hessian At a critical point (), examine the eigenvalues of : Eigenvalues  Classification --------------------------- All positive  Local minimum (bowl) All negative  Local maximum (hill) Mixed signs  Saddle point Any zero  Inconclusive 2×2 shortcut: At critical point : -  and : minimum -  and : maximum - : saddle point Pen & paper: → critical point at ,  → minimum ✓ Pen & paper: → critical point at → saddle point ✓ Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Backpropagation — computes the Jacobian-vector product efficiently (not the full Jacobian) - Newton's method — uses the Hessian for second-order optimisation: - Saddle points — a major challenge in deep learning; most critical points in high dimensions are saddle points, not minima - Change of variables — Jacobian determinant appears when transforming probability distributions (normalising flows) - Curvature — Hessian eigenvalues determine how "steep" vs "flat" the loss landscape is along each direction Matrix Calculus: Vector and Matrix Gradients Gradient of a scalar-valued function of a vector For a function , the gradient is the column vector of all partial derivatives: This is the direction of steepest ascent.  It points "uphill" in the input space. Common matrix calculus results These identities appear constantly in ML derivations.  Learn them like multiplication tables. Result 1: This is the vector analogue of . Pen & paper verification: Let , . , ✓ Result 2: If  is symmetric (), this simplifies to . This is the vector analogue of . Pen & paper verification (2×2 case): Let , Step 1 — expand : Step 2 — take partial derivatives: , Step 3 — verify with the formula: ✓ The total derivative and the Jacobian as best linear approximation The Jacobian isn't just a table of partial derivatives — it is the best linear approximation to the function near a point: This is the multivariable analogue of . The matrix  is the total derivative — it captures how all outputs change with respect to all inputs simultaneously. Tensor derivatives: gradient of a vector w.r.t. a vector When you differentiate a scalar w.r.t. a vector, you get a vector (the gradient). When you differentiate a vector w.r.t. a vector, you get a matrix — and that matrix is exactly the Jacobian: More generally: differentiating an order- tensor w.r.t. an order- tensor gives an order- tensor.  In deep learning, frameworks like PyTorch handle this bookkeeping automatically, but the principle is the same. Python Verification (Matrix Calculus) Inverse Function Theorem (brief) Statement If  is continuously differentiable at a point , and the Jacobian determinant is non-zero: then  is locally invertible near .  That is, there exists a neighbourhood of  where  exists and is also differentiable. The Jacobian of the inverse The Jacobian of the inverse function is the matrix inverse of the Jacobian: This is the multivariable version of the single-variable rule: if and , then . Intuition The Jacobian is the best linear approximation to .  If that linear map is invertible (non-zero determinant), then the function itself is invertible nearby.  A zero determinant means the linear approximation "crushes" some dimension — it loses information — so you cannot invert. title: Integrals — Riemann Sums to the Definite Integral
text: Integrals — Riemann Sums to the Definite Integral Intuition Integration is the reverse of differentiation.  If the derivative gives you the slope, the integral gives you the area under the curve.  It answers questions like: "What is the total distance travelled?" or "What is the probability that a value falls between 0 and 1?" title: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
text: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Intuition The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus is the bridge between derivatives and integrals — it says differentiation and integration are inverse operations. It's one of the most beautiful results in mathematics and the reason we can evaluate integrals using antiderivatives instead of computing limits of sums. Prerequisites - Tier 3, Lesson 2: Derivatives - Tier 3, Lesson 6: Integrals From First Principles Part 1 — Differentiation undoes integration If , then: In words: The derivative of "the area under  from  to " equals  itself. title: Multivariable Integration
text: Multivariable Integration Intuition Single-variable integrals give area under a curve.  Double integrals give the volume under a surface.  Triple integrals give… well, hyper-volume. These appear whenever you need to sum a quantity over a 2D or 3D region: probability over a 2D distribution, mass of a solid, or total light hitting a surface. title: Taylor and Maclaurin Series
text: Taylor and Maclaurin Series Intuition Any smooth function can be approximated by a polynomial — and polynomials are easy to compute (just multiply and add).  The Taylor series tells you exactly which polynomial, and how accurate the approximation is.  This is how calculators compute , , , and how we approximate complex functions in ML. title: Numerical vs Symbolic vs Automatic Differentiation
text: Numerical vs Symbolic vs Automatic Differentiation Intuition There are three fundamentally different ways to compute derivatives: symbolic (manipulate formulas like you do on paper), numerical (use the limit definition with small ), and automatic (track operations and apply the chain rule exactly).  PyTorch and JAX use automatic differentiation — it combines the best of both worlds. Prerequisites - Tier 3, Lesson 2: Derivatives - Tier 3, Lesson 4: Chain Rule From First Principles Method 1: Symbolic differentiation Apply derivative rules to produce an exact formula. Input: Output: Pros: Exact, gives a formula you can analyse. Cons: Expression can explode in size ("expression swell").  Requires a computer algebra system (SymPy, Mathematica). Method 2: Numerical differentiation Use the limit definition with a small : Forward difference: Central difference (more accurate): Pen & paper:  at , with : Forward: Central: Exact: .  Central difference is much more accurate! Pros: Works for any function, even black boxes. Cons: Rounding errors for very small , truncation errors for larger . Slow for many parameters (need one function call per parameter). Method 3: Automatic differentiation Track every arithmetic operation and apply the chain rule. Forward mode AD: Compute  and  simultaneously, propagating derivatives forward through the computation. Reverse mode AD: Compute  forward, then propagate derivatives backward (this IS backpropagation). Pen & paper: AD trace for  at Step  Value  Derivative (w.r.t. ) ------------------------------------- Exact:  ✓ Comparison Method  Exact?  Speed (n params)  Complexity --------------------------------------------- Symbolic  Yes  N/A  Expression swell Numerical  Approx   function calls  Simple but slow Forward AD  Yes   for  inputs  One pass per input Reverse AD  Yes   for  outputs  One backward pass for all inputs Why reverse mode wins for ML: A neural network has millions of inputs (weights) but typically one output (loss).  Reverse mode computes all gradients in a single backward pass. The right  for numerical differentiation Too large: truncation error dominates. Too small: rounding error dominates (floating-point noise). Optimal  for forward difference. Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - PyTorch autograd — reverse-mode AD; builds a computation graph and runs backward pass - JAX — uses forward and reverse mode AD, with JIT compilation - Gradient checking — use numerical differentiation to verify your analytical/AD gradients (common debugging technique) - SymPy — Python library for symbolic differentiation - Finite elements — numerical methods for PDEs use numerical differentiation Check Your Understanding 1. (Hint: Taylor-expand  and  and subtract.) 3. Pen & paper: Trace the forward-mode AD computation for  at . 4. Think about it: A neural network has 10 million weights and 1 loss value.  Why is reverse-mode AD vastly more efficient than forward-mode?

title: Sample Spaces, Events, and Probability Axioms
text: Sample Spaces, Events, and Probability Axioms Intuition Probability quantifies uncertainty.  When you flip a coin, draw a card, or train a neural network on random mini-batches, you're dealing with randomness.  The axioms of probability are the three simple rules from which all of probability theory follows. Prerequisites - Tier 1, Lesson 2: Set Theory (unions, intersections, complements) - Tier 1, Lesson 4: Combinatorics (counting) From First Principles Sample space The sample space  is the set of all possible outcomes. title: Conditional Probability and Bayes' Theorem
text: Conditional Probability and Bayes' Theorem Intuition Conditional probability asks: "Given that I know something happened, how does that change the probability of something else?"  Bayes' Theorem flips the direction: if you know , it tells you .  It's the mathematical foundation of spam filters, medical diagnosis, and Bayesian machine learning. Prerequisites - Tier 4, Lesson 1: Probability Axioms (events, independence) From First Principles Conditional probability Read: "probability of A, given B." Pen & paper: Roll a die.   = "roll a 6",  = "roll even" = . Knowing the roll is even reduces the sample space to 3 outcomes. Multiplication rule Rearranging: Pen & paper: Bag with 5 red, 3 blue.  Draw 2 without replacement. Law of total probability If  partition : Pen & paper: A factory has 2 machines.  Machine 1 makes 60% of products (3% defect rate).  Machine 2 makes 40% (5% defect rate). 3.8% overall defect rate. Bayes' Theorem Or with the law of total probability in the denominator: Deriving Bayes' Theorem (pen & paper) Start from: Solve for : That's it. Pen & paper: Medical test - Disease prevalence:  (1 in 100) - Test sensitivity:  (catches 99% of sick people) - Test specificity:  →  (5% false positive) Question: You test positive.  What is ? Only 16.7%!  Despite a 99% sensitive test, most positives are false positives because the disease is rare.  This is called the base rate fallacy. Pen & paper: Which machine made the defective product? From the factory example, a product is defective.  Which machine made it? Machine 2 is slightly more likely — it has a higher defect rate despite lower volume. Bayesian terminology Term  Symbol  Meaning ----------------------- Prior    Belief before seeing evidence Likelihood    How probable the evidence is, given the hypothesis Evidence    Total probability of the evidence Posterior    Updated belief after seeing evidence Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Spam filters — Naive Bayes: - Medical AI — diagnostic systems use Bayes' theorem with test results as evidence - Bayesian inference — update beliefs as new data arrives (online learning) - Bayesian neural networks — place probability distributions over weights - A/B testing — Bayesian A/B tests compute - Game AI — update probability of enemy position given observed clues Check Your Understanding 1. title: Random Variables — Discrete and Continuous
text: Random Variables — Discrete and Continuous Intuition A random variable is a number that depends on the outcome of a random experiment.  "The sum of two dice" is a random variable.  "The height of a randomly chosen person" is a random variable.  Random variables let us do algebra with randomness — add them, multiply them, compute averages. Prerequisites - Tier 4, Lesson 1: Probability Axioms From First Principles Definition A random variable  is a function from the sample space to the real numbers: Notation: uppercase  for the variable, lowercase  for a specific value. title: Expectation, Variance, and Standard Deviation
text: Expectation, Variance, and Standard Deviation Intuition Expectation is the "average" value you'd get if you repeated the experiment infinitely many times.  Variance measures how spread out the values are around that average.  Standard deviation is variance in the same units as the original data.  These three numbers summarise any distribution. Prerequisites - Tier 4, Lesson 3: Random Variables (PMF, PDF) From First Principles Expected value (mean) Discrete: Continuous: Pen & paper: Expected value of a die roll Note: 3.5 is not a possible outcome! The expectation doesn't have to be a possible value. Pen & paper: Expected sum of two dice This is why 7 is the most likely sum and the centre of the distribution. Properties of expectation Property  Formula ------------------- Linearity Sum   (always, even if dependent!) Product (independent)   (only if independent) Constant Pen & paper:  where  is a die roll Variance The second formula is usually easier for pen & paper. Pen & paper: Variance of a die roll First: Standard deviation For a die: Properties of variance Property  Formula ------------------- Scaling Shift   (adding a constant doesn't change spread) Sum (independent) Non-negative Pen & paper: Continuous example on . Expected value of a function This is called the law of the unconscious statistician (LOTUS). Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Loss functions — expected loss over the data distribution is what we minimise - Batch normalisation — normalises using running mean and variance - Bias-variance tradeoff — high variance = overfitting, high bias = underfitting - Risk assessment — expected value of losses, variance = risk - Game design — expected reward per action, variance affects player experience - Random variable arithmetic — propagating uncertainty through computations Moments What are moments? Moments are a systematic way to describe the shape of a distribution. Think of them as increasingly detailed measurements: the first moment tells you where the distribution is centred, the second tells you how wide it is, the third tells you whether it leans, and the fourth tells you how heavy the tails are. Raw moments The -th (raw) moment of : - :  — the mean - :  — appears in the variance formula Central moments The -th central moment (centred around the mean): - :  (always zero — deviations cancel) - :  — the variance - : relates to skewness (asymmetry).  Positive → right tail longer; negative → left tail longer.  Normalised skewness: - : relates to kurtosis (tail heaviness).  Normalised: .  The normal distribution has ; "excess kurtosis" is . title: Discrete Distributions — Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson
text: Discrete Distributions — Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson Intuition Certain patterns of randomness appear again and again.  Coin flips → Bernoulli. "How many successes in  trials?" → Binomial.  "How many events in a time window?" → Poisson.  Each of these models a counting process — the outcome is always a non-negative integer. Prerequisites - Tier 4, Lesson 4: Expectation and Variance From First Principles Bernoulli distribution A single trial with probability  of success. , Pen & paper: Fair coin: , , . Binomial distribution independent Bernoulli trials.   = number of successes. , Pen & paper: Flip a coin 10 times. ? Poisson distribution Models rare events in a fixed interval.  Parameter  = average rate. , Pen & paper: A server gets 4 requests/second on average.  ? Poisson as limit of Binomial When  is large and  is small, with  held constant: Pen & paper:  light bulbs, each fails with .  . Geometric distribution Models the number of trials until the first success. , Intuition: "How many coin flips until I get the first heads?" With : on average 2 flips. With : on average 100. Memoryless property: Past failures don't change the probability of future success. The Geometric distribution is the only discrete distribution with this property. Pen & paper: A network packet has  chance of arriving. How many attempts on average? What is ? attempts on average. (success on first try) Pen & paper: CDF (probability of success within  trials): For our example:  (67.2% chance within 5 attempts). Summary table Distribution  Parameters ------------------------------------------------- Bernoulli Binomial Geometric Poisson Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Bernoulli — dropout (each neuron is a Bernoulli trial), click-through rates - Binomial — "how many of  users convert?" in A/B testing - Poisson — server request rates, rare event modelling, network packet arrivals - Geometric — retry loops (expected retries until success), hash table probe counts, coupon collector analysis, expected time to find a free slot - Loss functions — cross-entropy loss assumes Bernoulli-distributed outcomes Check Your Understanding 1. title: Continuous Distributions — Uniform, Gaussian, Exponential, Gamma, Chi-Square, Student's t
text: Continuous Distributions — Uniform, Gaussian, Exponential, Gamma, Chi-Square, Student's t Intuition Continuous random variables can take any real value in some interval.  "Any value equally likely?" → Uniform.  "Nature's default bell curve" → Gaussian. "Time until the next event" → Exponential.  "Sum of waiting times" → Gamma. "Sum of squared normals" → Chi-square.  "Small-sample estimate of a mean" → Student's t.  These distributions are the building blocks of statistical inference and machine learning. Prerequisites - Tier 4, Lesson 4: Expectation and Variance - Tier 4, Lesson 5: Discrete Distributions - Tier 3, Lesson 6: Integrals (area under a curve) From First Principles Continuous Uniform distribution All values in  equally likely.  The PDF is a flat rectangle. Deriving the moments: Pen & paper: : , . Gaussian (Normal) distribution — the bell curve , Standard normal: , written . The 68-95-99.7 rule (memorise this): - 68% of values fall within - 95% within - 99.7% within Pen & paper: Heights are  cm. - 68% between 160 and 180 cm - 95% between 150 and 190 cm Why Gaussians are everywhere: The Central Limit Theorem (Lesson 7) explains it: the sum of many independent random effects tends toward a Gaussian, regardless of the individual distributions. Exponential distribution Models the waiting time until the next event in a Poisson process. If events arrive at rate  per unit time, the time  between consecutive events follows: Derivation from Poisson: The probability of no events in time  is .  So , giving CDF and PDF . Moments: Memoryless property: .  Past waiting gives no information about future waiting — unique to the exponential. Pen & paper: Server requests arrive at /minute.  Mean wait minutes  seconds. . Gamma distribution Generalises the exponential: the time until the -th event. where  is the gamma function.  For positive integers, . , Special case:  gives the exponential distribution. Pen & paper: Time until the 3rd customer, , /hour. hours,  hours. Chi-square distribution If  are independent standard normals, then: This is a Gamma distribution with  and . , Pen & paper:  degrees of freedom.  , . Why it matters: Sample variance follows a chi-square distribution (after scaling).  If : where . Student's t distribution When estimating a mean from a small sample and variance is unknown: The PDF is: where  is degrees of freedom. (for ),  (for ) Key properties: - Symmetric about 0, like the normal - Heavier tails than normal (more probability in the extremes) - As , Pen & paper: :  (compared to 1 for the normal — fatter tails).  : (almost normal). Summary table Distribution  Parameters ------------------------------------------------- Uniform Gaussian Exponential Gamma Chi-square Student's Visualisation The matplotlib code below plots all six distributions on a single figure for visual comparison.  Notice how the t distribution's tails are heavier than the normal, and how chi-square is right-skewed. Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Uniform — random number generation, initialising neural network weights - Gaussian — weight initialisation (Xavier, He), noise in GANs, Gaussian processes - Exponential — modelling inter-arrival times in queuing systems, network traffic - Gamma — Bayesian prior for rate parameters, modelling wait times - Chi-square — goodness-of-fit tests, feature selection in NLP - Student's t — confidence intervals with small samples, robust loss functions (t-distribution loss) - Loss functions — MSE loss assumes Gaussian errors; heavy-tailed losses use t-distributions Check Your Understanding 1. title: Central Limit Theorem
text: Central Limit Theorem Intuition Take any distribution — no matter how weird — and average many independent samples from it.  That average will be approximately normally distributed. The more samples, the better the approximation.  This is why the bell curve appears everywhere in nature and why statistical tests work. Prerequisites - Tier 4, Lesson 6: Continuous Distributions (Gaussian) - Tier 4, Lesson 4: Expectation and Variance From First Principles Statement If  are independent random variables from any distribution with mean  and variance , then as : Or equivalently, the standardised version: Key points 1. The original distribution can be anything: uniform, exponential, Poisson, bimodal... 2. The mean  is approximately normal for large enough 3. The spread of  shrinks as  — the standard error 4. Rule of thumb:  usually suffices Pen & paper: Die rolls A single die roll: , , . Average of  rolls: - Mean of : - Standard error: By CLT: From the standard normal table: Why ? So to halve the standard error, you need 4× the samples.  To get 10× more precise, you need 100× the samples. Where CLT breaks down - Distributions with infinite variance (e.g., Cauchy distribution) - Dependent samples - Very small  for highly skewed distributions Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Mini-batch SGD — the gradient estimate from a mini-batch is approximately normal (by CLT), enabling convergence analysis - Confidence intervals — "the true mean is within " with 95% confidence - A/B testing — sample means are normal → we can use z-tests and t-tests - Monte Carlo — error decreases as , directly from CLT - Why Gaussian noise — many real-world noise sources are sums of many small effects → CLT → Gaussian Check Your Understanding 1. Pen & paper: You average 100 rolls of a fair die.  What is the standard error of the mean?  What is  approximately? 2. title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)
text: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) Intuition You observe data.  You have a model with unknown parameters.  MLE asks: "What parameter values make the observed data most probable?"  It's the most fundamental method for fitting models to data, and it's what happens under the hood when you train a neural network with cross-entropy loss. Prerequisites - Tier 4, Lessons 5-6: Discrete and Continuous Distributions (Bernoulli, Gaussian) - Tier 3, Lesson 2: Derivatives (finding maxima) From First Principles The likelihood function Given data  and a model with parameter : (Assuming independent observations.) The log-likelihood Products are hard; sums are easy.  Take the log: Since  is monotonically increasing, maximising  is the same as maximising . The MLE recipe 1. Write the log-likelihood 2. Take the derivative: 3. Set it to zero: 4. Solve for  (the MLE estimate) Pen & paper: MLE for Bernoulli (coin flip) Data:  flips,  heads.  Model: . The MLE is just the sample proportion! Pen & paper: 7 heads in 10 flips → . Pen & paper: MLE for Gaussian mean Data:  from  (assume  known). The MLE for the Gaussian mean is the sample mean. Pen & paper: MLE for Gaussian variance Now let both  and  be unknown. Note: this is the biased estimator (divides by , not ). MLE and cross-entropy loss For classification with labels  and predicted probabilities : Maximising this = minimising the binary cross-entropy loss: MLE is what you're doing when training a neural network with cross-entropy loss. Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Neural network training — cross-entropy loss IS negative log-likelihood (MLE) - Logistic regression — MLE of Bernoulli model with log-odds = linear function - Language models — trained by maximising likelihood of the training text - Gaussian mixture models — MLE via the EM algorithm - Bayesian ML — MLE is the "MAP estimate with a flat prior" Check Your Understanding 1. title: Information Theory — Entropy, Cross-Entropy, KL Divergence
text: Information Theory — Entropy, Cross-Entropy, KL Divergence Intuition Entropy measures how "surprised" you are on average.  A fair coin has high entropy (you can't predict it).  A biased coin with  has low entropy (boring — you always know what's coming).  Cross-entropy measures how well your predictions match reality.  KL divergence measures how different two distributions are.  These concepts power every classification loss function in deep learning. Prerequisites - Tier 4, Lessons 5-6: Discrete and Continuous Distributions - Tier 4, Lesson 8: MLE (log-likelihood) From First Principles Entropy For a discrete distribution  over outcomes : (Using natural log.  If using , the unit is bits.) Pen & paper: Entropy of a fair coin In bits: Pen & paper: Biased coin () Less entropy = more predictable. Cross-entropy How many bits/nats to encode data from distribution  using a code optimised for distribution : Always  — you can't do better than knowing the true distribution. Pen & paper: Binary cross-entropy True label , predicted probability : If  (bad prediction): Much higher — bad predictions are penalised heavily. KL divergence How much extra information is needed when using  instead of : Properties: -  (Gibbs' inequality) -  iff - NOT symmetric:  in general Pen & paper: KL divergence , : Cross-entropy as a loss function For multi-class classification with  classes: - One-hot encoding: The true label is a vector  with a 1 in the true class position and 0s elsewhere.  E.g., class 2 of 3: . - Softmax: Converts raw scores (logits)  into probabilities: .  All outputs are positive and sum to 1. With true distribution  (one-hot) and predicted  (softmax output): Since  is one-hot (only one class  has ): Minimising cross-entropy = maximising log-likelihood = MLE. Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Cross-entropy loss — THE standard classification loss function - Softmax + cross-entropy — combined for multi-class neural network training - Language models — perplexity =  measures model quality - Data compression — entropy = theoretical minimum bits per symbol - KL divergence — used in VAEs (variational autoencoders), policy gradient methods, knowledge distillation - Mutual information — : how much knowing  tells you about Check Your Understanding 1. Pen & paper: Compute  for  and . 4. Think about it: Why is KL divergence not symmetric?  Give an intuitive example of when  and  would differ significantly.

title: Covariance and Correlation Matrices
text: Covariance and Correlation Matrices Intuition Covariance tells you whether two variables tend to move together (positive), move opposite (negative), or are unrelated (near zero).  The correlation normalises this to  so you can compare across different scales. The covariance matrix packages all pairwise relationships into one matrix — and it's the starting point for PCA. Prerequisites - Tier 4, Lesson 4: Expectation and Variance - Tier 2, Lesson 1: Matrices From First Principles Covariance Interpretation: - :  and  increase together - : when  increases,  tends to decrease - : no linear relationship (but could still be non-linearly related!) Note: Pen & paper: Compute covariance Data:  = , product ----------------------------------------- 1  2  -3  -4  12 3  4  -1  -2  2 5  8  1  2  2 7  10  3  4  12 (Dividing by  for sample covariance.) Correlation coefficient (Pearson's ) This normalises to : - : perfect positive linear relationship - : perfect negative linear relationship - : no linear relationship Pen & paper (continuing): , , Nearly perfect positive correlation — the data is almost perfectly linear. Covariance matrix For a dataset with  features, the covariance matrix  is : Diagonal entries = variances, off-diagonal = covariances. Pen & paper: For our data with features  and : Properties - Symmetric: - Positive semi-definite: all eigenvalues - Diagonal entries: variances (always ) - If features are independent: covariance matrix is diagonal Correlation matrix Normalise each entry: Diagonal entries are all 1. Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - PCA — eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix gives principal components - Feature selection — highly correlated features are redundant - Portfolio theory — covariance between stock returns determines diversification - Mahalanobis distance — uses the inverse covariance matrix: - Gaussian processes — defined entirely by mean and covariance functions - Batch normalisation — sometimes uses whitening (decorrelation via covariance matrix) Check Your Understanding 1. title: Joint Distributions — Joint PMF/PDF, Marginals, and Independence
text: Joint Distributions — Joint PMF/PDF, Marginals, and Independence Intuition So far every distribution has described a single variable.  But in the real world variables come in groups: a pixel has red, green, and blue channels; a data point has height and weight; a neural network layer has hundreds of activations.  A joint distribution describes the probability of two (or more) variables simultaneously.  From the joint, you can recover each variable's individual (marginal) distribution, condition on one to learn about the other, and test whether they are truly independent. Prerequisites - Tier 4, Lesson 4: Expectation and Variance - Tier 4, Lessons 5-6: Discrete and Continuous Distributions - Tier 3, Lesson 6: Integrals (for continuous case) From First Principles Discrete case: Joint PMF For two discrete random variables  and , the joint PMF is: It must satisfy: 1.  for all 2. title: Expectation and Joint Distributions
text: Expectation and Joint Distributions Intuition When you have two random variables, how do you compute the expected value of their sum?  Their product?  What if you only know one of them — can you still say something about the other?  These questions are answered by the linearity of expectation, the product rule for independent variables, and conditional expectation.  These tools are essential for analysing anything from portfolio returns to neural network outputs. Prerequisites - Tier 4, Lesson 4: Expectation and Variance - Tier 4, Lesson 11: Joint Distributions (joint PMF/PDF, marginals) From First Principles Expectation of a function of two variables For discrete : For continuous: Linearity of expectation For any random variables  and  (independent or not): Proof (discrete case): This extends to any finite sum: . title: Bivariate Normal Distribution
text: Bivariate Normal Distribution Intuition The bivariate normal is the two-dimensional bell curve.  Imagine a hill of sand on a table: viewed from above the contour lines are ellipses.  If the two variables are uncorrelated the ellipses align with the axes; as correlation increases the ellipses tilt.  This distribution appears whenever two quantities are jointly influenced by many small, additive effects — heights and weights of people, returns of two correlated stocks, or two channels in a sensor reading. Prerequisites - Tier 4, Lesson 6: Continuous Distributions (Gaussian) - Tier 4, Lesson 10: Covariance and Correlation - Tier 4, Lesson 11: Joint Distributions (joint PDF, marginals) - Tier 2, Lesson 7: Determinants (for the matrix in the exponent) From First Principles The PDF For random vector  with means , standard deviations , and correlation : where the quadratic form is: Matrix form Define  and covariance matrix . Then: where . Pen & paper: Specific example Let , , , , . Evaluate at : Marginal distributions are normal A key property: if  is bivariate normal, then: The marginals are simply the individual normals — the correlation only affects the joint behaviour, not the individual distributions. Pen & paper verification: Integrate out : This is exactly . Conditional distributions are normal Given , the conditional distribution of  is: Pen & paper: With , , : Knowing  shifts the expected value of  from 0 to 1 and reduces its variance. Contour ellipses The level curves  are ellipses.  The equation  (for constant ) defines an ellipse whose: - Axes are aligned with the eigenvectors of - Axis lengths are proportional to  (square roots of eigenvalues) - Tilt angle is determined by : when , axes are horizontal/vertical Pen & paper: For : Eigenvalues:  or . Eigenvector for :  — the 45-degree direction. The contour ellipses are tilted 45 degrees, stretched along the  direction. Uncorrelated does not always mean independent — except for the bivariate normal For general distributions,  does NOT imply independence. But for the bivariate normal,  DOES imply independence. This is a special and important property. Visualisation The contour plot shows ellipses at probability levels.  With  the ellipses are axis-aligned circles (when ).  As increases, they stretch into tilted ellipses.  At  the ellipse collapses to a line (perfect linear relationship). Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) — each cluster is a multivariate normal; the bivariate case is the simplest non-trivial example - Kalman filters — state estimation uses multivariate normals; the bivariate case arises in 2D tracking (position/velocity) - PCA — principal components are eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, which for bivariate normal data gives the ellipse axes - Variational autoencoders (VAEs) — the latent space is often modelled as multivariate normal - Gaussian processes — defined by mean and covariance; the bivariate case is the building block Check Your Understanding 1. title: Sampling Distributions
text: Sampling Distributions Intuition Every time you compute a sample mean from data, you get a slightly different number.  That sample mean is itself a random variable with its own distribution — the sampling distribution.  Understanding sampling distributions is the bridge between "I have data" and "I can make inferences about the population." It answers the question: how much should I trust a single sample's statistics? Prerequisites - Tier 4, Lesson 4: Expectation and Variance - Tier 4, Lesson 6: Continuous Distributions (Gaussian, Chi-square, Student's t) - Tier 4, Lesson 7: Central Limit Theorem From First Principles Statistics are random variables A statistic is any function of the data: the sample mean , sample variance , sample median, etc.  Because the data is random, so is the statistic.  Its probability distribution is called its sampling distribution. Sampling distribution of the mean If  are i.i.d. with mean  and variance : Pen & paper derivation: (Uses independence for the variance step.) Standard error The standard error (SE) is the standard deviation of : Pen & paper: Population with . - : SE - : SE - : SE To halve the SE, you need  the sample size. When the population is normal If , then exactly (not just approximately): This holds for any , not just large . Connection to the CLT If the population is not normal, the CLT tells us that for large : The sampling distribution of the mean is approximately normal regardless of the population shape, provided  is large enough (typically ). Sampling distribution of the variance If , the sample variance is: Then: Pen & paper derivation of moments: (unbiased estimator — this is why we divide by !) Pen & paper: , : , , The t-distribution connection When  is unknown (the typical case), we replace it with : Pen & paper:  observations. - If we knew : - Since we estimate  with : The  distribution has heavier tails, reflecting the extra uncertainty from estimating . Pen & paper: Complete worked example IQ scores: , .  Sample of . There's only a 2.3% chance of getting a sample mean above 105 from a population with true mean 100. Visualisation Plotting the sampling distribution for increasing  shows it narrowing around .  The spread shrinks as : from  (the original wide distribution) to  (a tight spike around ). Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - A/B testing — "is the difference in means real?" requires knowing the sampling distribution of - Mini-batch SGD — the gradient from a mini-batch is a sample mean; its SE determines training stability - Ensemble methods — averaging  models reduces variance by  (same  principle) - Monte Carlo estimation — error bars on Monte Carlo estimates use the standard error - Bootstrap — resampling to empirically estimate the sampling distribution of any statistic Check Your Understanding 1. title: Confidence Intervals
text: Confidence Intervals Intuition A sample mean is almost never exactly equal to the population mean.  A confidence interval gives a range that, if you repeated the experiment many times, would contain the true mean a specified fraction of the time (say 95%). It answers the practical question every data scientist asks: "I computed a number from data — how much should I trust it?" Prerequisites - Tier 4, Lesson 6: Continuous Distributions (Gaussian, Student's t) - Tier 4, Lesson 7: Central Limit Theorem - Tier 4, Lesson 14: Sampling Distributions (standard error, t-distribution) From First Principles The idea We know  (exactly for normal data, approximately by CLT).  So: Rearranging for : Case 1: CI for the mean,  known (z-interval) For 95% confidence: . For 99%: . Pen & paper: IQ scores, .  Sample: , . Interpretation: If we repeated this experiment many times, 95% of the resulting intervals would contain the true .  This particular interval either contains  or it does not — probability applies to the procedure, not to this single interval. Case 2: CI for the mean,  unknown (t-interval) Replace  with  and use the -distribution with  degrees of freedom: Pen & paper: A sample of  exam scores: , . For 95% CI with : Compare: if we had used , we'd get  — narrower, but wrong because it underestimates the uncertainty from estimating . Case 3: CI for a proportion If  is a sample proportion (e.g., fraction of users who clicked): Pen & paper: Survey of  users, 120 clicked.  . Width of the confidence interval The CI width is .  It depends on: 1. Confidence level — higher confidence → wider interval ( gets larger) 2. Sample size — larger  → narrower interval (SE shrinks as ) 3. Population variability — larger  → wider interval Pen & paper: Choosing sample size Want a 95% CI for a mean with width  (i.e., margin of error ), : Common misinterpretations (avoid these!) - Wrong: "There's a 95% probability that  is in this interval." is fixed; it either is or is not in the interval. - Right: "If we repeated the experiment many times, 95% of intervals would contain ." - Wrong: "95% of the data falls in this interval." (That's a prediction interval, not a confidence interval.) Pen & paper: Comparing two methods Method A: , ,  → SE , 95% CI Method B: , ,  → SE , 95% CI Same point estimate, but method B's interval is twice as wide.  Quadrupling the sample size halved the width. Visualisation A classic "confidence interval coverage" plot: generate 100 samples, compute 95% CIs, and show them as horizontal bars.  About 95 of them cross the true line; the remaining ~5 miss it.  This is what "95% confidence" means. Python Verification Connection to CS / Games / AI - A/B testing — "is the conversion rate significantly different?" is answered by checking if CIs overlap (or better, by CI for the difference) - Model evaluation — report accuracy as  margin, not just a single number - Hyperparameter tuning — CIs on validation metrics tell you whether differences are real or noise - Reinforcement learning — confidence bounds drive exploration (UCB algorithm: pick action with highest upper confidence bound) - Monte Carlo estimates — error bars on simulated quantities are confidence intervals - Bayesian vs frequentist — CIs are frequentist; the Bayesian analogue is the credible interval Check Your Understanding 1. title: Objective Functions and Loss Functions
text: Objective Functions and Loss Functions Intuition An objective function is what you want to minimise (or maximise).  In ML, this is the loss function — a single number that tells you how wrong your model's predictions are.  Training a model = finding parameters that make this number as small as possible.  Different problems need different loss functions, and the choice has deep mathematical consequences. Prerequisites - Tier 3, Lesson 2: Derivatives (finding minima) - Tier 4, Lesson 8: MLE (connection to cross-entropy) From First Principles The setup Given: - Data:  for - Model:  with parameters - Loss: Goal: Find Mean Squared Error (MSE) Pen & paper: Predictions: , targets: . Properties: - Robust to outliers (linear penalty, not quadratic) - Not differentiable at 0 (use subgradients) - MLE under Laplace noise assumption Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) For binary classification (, ): Pen & paper: : : — much higher penalty for confident wrong prediction! Derivative: Categorical Cross-Entropy For multi-class ( classes, one-hot , softmax output ): Since  is one-hot with true class : Pen & paper: True class 2, softmax output : Comparison table Loss  When to use  Outlier robust?  Probabilistic interpretation ---------------------------------------------------------------- MSE  Regression  No  Gaussian noise MAE  Regression (robust)  Yes  Laplace noise Huber  Regression (compromise)  Partly  — BCE  Binary classification  —  Bernoulli MLE Cross-Entropy  Multi-class classification  —  Categorical MLE Huber loss (pen & paper) Quadratic for small errors (like MSE), linear for large errors (like MAE). title: Gradient Descent — The Algorithm Derived from Calculus
text: Gradient Descent — The Algorithm Derived from Calculus Intuition Imagine you're blindfolded on a hilly landscape and want to find the lowest point.  You feel the slope under your feet and take a step downhill.  Repeat. That's gradient descent: measure the gradient (slope), step in the opposite direction, repeat until you reach a minimum. Prerequisites - Tier 3, Lesson 3: Partial Derivatives and Gradient - Tier 5, Lesson 1: Loss Functions From First Principles The update rule - : parameters (weights) - : learning rate (step size) - : gradient of the loss Why "negative gradient"? points in the direction of steepest increase.  We want to decrease the loss, so we go in the opposite direction. Pen & paper: 1D gradient descent Minimise . Start at , learning rate : Step        Update ------------------------------------ 0  0  9  -6 1  0.6  5.76  -4.8 2  1.08  3.69  -3.84 3  1.464  2.36  -3.072 Converging toward  (the minimum). Pen & paper: 2D gradient descent Minimise  (an elliptical bowl). Start at , : Step 0: Step 1: Learning rate matters Too large (): For  at : ,  — oscillates forever! Too small (): Converges, but takes thousands of steps. Just right: Depends on the curvature of the loss landscape. Convergence condition For convex  with Lipschitz gradient (bounded second derivative ): Gradient descent converges if . For : , so need . Batch gradient descent Use the full dataset to compute the gradient at each step: Pros: Stable, smooth convergence. Cons: Slow for large datasets (must process all  samples per step).
